exampleSentences: The new government promised sweeping prison reform to reduce reoffending rates.

exampleSentences: It is every citizen's responsibility to vote in a democratic election.

exampleSentences: Freedom of the press allows journalists to report on government corruption without fear.

exampleSentences: The United Nations was founded partly to protect human rights across all nations.

exampleSentences: The teenage activist gave a speech demanding stronger climate laws at the summit.

exampleSentences: Moving to a bigger city gave her the opportunity to find work in her field.

exampleSentences: Many families faced financial hardship after the factory shut down unexpectedly.

words.jpTranslation: 克服する(こくふくする)

exampleSentences: Burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide, a major driver of global warming.

exampleSentences: Our school recycles glass bottles and aluminum cans every Friday.

exampleSentences: The city produced so much food waste that it began converting it into biogas.

words.jpTranslation: 再利用可能な(さいりようかのうな)

words.reading: グリーンハウス・エフェクト

words.reading: ナチュラル・リソーシズ

exampleSentences: The recommendation algorithm decides which videos appear on your home screen.

words.reading: アーティフィシャル・インテリジェンス

exampleSentences: Automation in car factories lets robots weld frames around the clock without breaks.

exampleSentences: The team held a virtual meeting because members were spread across five countries.

exampleSentences: The fitness app collects data on your sleep patterns and heart rate each night.

exampleSentences: Many newspapers shifted to digital editions as print circulation continued to fall.

exampleSentences: The hospital's billing software crashed during the busiest hour of the morning shift.

exampleSentences: The old laptop struggled because its hardware could not handle modern video editing.

exampleSentences: The office network slowed dramatically when everyone joined the video call at once.

exampleSentences: The library's database holds records of every book borrowed over the past thirty years.

words.reading: サイバーセキュリティ

exampleSentences: He taught himself programming at sixteen and built a budgeting app by eighteen.

exampleSentences: Medical technology has made it possible to detect cancer at much earlier stages.

exampleSentences: Engineers tested the prototype in a wind tunnel before committing to full production.

exampleSentences: The school upgraded its computers so students could run modern design software.

exampleSentences: She downloaded the report as a PDF so she could read it on the train.

exampleSentences: He uploaded his portfolio to the design platform and received client enquiries the same day.

words.jpTranslation: 奨励策(しょうれいさく)

exampleSentences: Short breaks during the workday can actually boost productivity rather than reducing it.

exampleSentences: LED lighting improved the building's energy efficiency and cut the electricity bill by a third.

exampleSentences: Her main motivation for studying abroad was to become fluent in a second language.

exampleSentences: The teacher addressed the student's disruptive behavior calmly and without embarrassing him.

exampleSentences: Public perception of the politician changed dramatically after the scandal became headline news.

exampleSentences: Regular puzzles and reading can slow cognitive decline in older adults.

exampleSentences: His voice cracked with emotion as he described returning home after ten years abroad.

exampleSentences: He pulled the child back from the road purely on instinct, without thinking at all.

exampleSentences: Checking his phone first thing in the morning had become a habit he wanted to break.

words.reading: リインフォースメント

words.englishDefinition: the subjects and content taught in a school or educational program

words.jpTranslation: 奨学金(しょうがくきん)
exampleSentences: She received a full scholarship that covered tuition, housing, and living expenses.
The university offers merit-based scholarships to students with outstanding academic records.
words.englishDefinition: a grant of money awarded to a student to help pay for their education

words.reading: クォリフィケーション
exampleSentences: The position requires a professional qualification in accounting or a related field.
He gained his teaching qualification after completing a one-year postgraduate program.
words.englishDefinition: an official certificate or skill that shows you are trained to do a particular job

exampleSentences: She earned a degree in environmental science from a university in Tokyo.
Many employers now require at least a bachelor's degree for entry-level positions.
words.englishDefinition: an academic qualification awarded by a university upon completing a course of study

words.jpTranslation: 授業料(じゅぎょうりょう)
exampleSentences: Annual tuition at private universities has risen sharply over the past decade.
He took out a loan to cover his tuition after his scholarship was cut.
words.englishDefinition: the fee charged by a school or university for teaching and instruction

exampleSentences: The university's campus spans over 200 hectares and includes labs, gyms, and dormitories.
Students gather on campus every Friday afternoon for the outdoor market.
words.englishDefinition: the grounds and buildings of a university or college

exampleSentences: The professor gave a lecture on climate policy to an audience of three hundred students.
She records every lecture so she can review the material before exams.
words.englishDefinition: a formal talk given to a class or audience, typically at a university

exampleSentences: The weekly seminar gave students a chance to discuss the readings in small groups.
He attended a weekend seminar on negotiation skills run by a business school.
words.englishDefinition: a small-group class or discussion session focused on a particular topic

exampleSentences: Her master's thesis examined how social media affects teenage self-esteem.
He spent two years gathering data before he started writing his thesis.
words.englishDefinition: a long research paper written to earn a university degree, especially at master's level

words.jpTranslation: 博士論文(はかせろんぶん)
exampleSentences: Her doctoral dissertation on colonial literature was later published as a book.

words.englishDefinition: a long, original research document submitted to earn a doctoral degree

words.jpTranslation: 学術的な(がくじゅつてきな)
exampleSentences: His academic record was strong enough to earn him a place at a top university.
She pursued an academic career, eventually becoming a professor of linguistics.
words.englishDefinition: relating to education, study, or scholarly research at a school or university

exampleSentences: Her grade on the final essay pulled her semester average up significantly.
Students who miss more than three classes risk receiving a failing grade.
words.englishDefinition: a mark or score that shows how well a student performed on a test or course

exampleSentences: The teacher used a short quiz as a formative assessment to check understanding.
Continuous assessment across the semester replaced the traditional end-of-year exam.
words.englishDefinition: a process of judging a student's knowledge, skills, or performance

exampleSentences: Enrollment in online degree programs has doubled since the pandemic began.
Students must complete enrollment before the start of each academic semester.
words.englishDefinition: the process of officially registering to join a school, course, or program

exampleSentences: Her entire family flew in from overseas to attend her graduation ceremony.
After graduation, he plans to work abroad before applying to graduate school.
words.englishDefinition: the ceremony or event marking the successful completion of a degree or school program

exampleSentences: Financial hardship is the most common reason for dropout among first-year students.
The government launched a program to reduce the high school dropout rate in rural areas.
words.englishDefinition: someone who leaves school or a course before completing it, or the act of doing so

words.jpTranslation: 識字能力(しきじのうりょく)
exampleSentences: The charity works in developing regions to raise literacy rates among adult women.
Digital literacy is now considered as fundamental as reading and writing.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to read and write

words.jpTranslation: 数的能力(すうてきのうりょく)
exampleSentences: Strong numeracy allows people to manage budgets, interpret statistics, and spot errors.
The program targets children aged six to ten to build numeracy before middle school.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to understand and work with numbers and basic mathematics

words.jpTranslation: 教授法(きょうじゅほう)
exampleSentences: Project-based learning is a pedagogy that encourages students to solve real-world problems.
Teacher training programs focus heavily on pedagogy, not just subject knowledge.
words.englishDefinition: the theory and practice of how teaching and learning should be carried out

exampleSentences: Her mentor in the research lab showed her how to design experiments rigorously.
He credits his high school mentor with encouraging him to pursue engineering.
words.englishDefinition: an experienced person who gives guidance and advice to someone less experienced

words.jpTranslation: 個別指導(こべつしどう)
exampleSentences: After-school tutoring helped him catch up in algebra and boost his confidence.
Many families rely on private tutoring to prepare children for entrance exams.
words.englishDefinition: one-on-one or small-group teaching given to help a student understand a subject

exampleSentences: The company switched to e-learning so employees could train at their own pace.
E-learning platforms let students access video lectures from anywhere in the world.
words.englishDefinition: learning that takes place through digital devices and the internet

words.reading: ブレンデッド・ラーニング
words.jpTranslation: ブレンド型学習(ブレンドがたがくしゅう)
exampleSentences: The school adopted blended learning, mixing face-to-face classes with online modules.
Blended learning allows students who miss a lesson to catch up via recorded content.
words.englishDefinition: an educational approach that combines in-person classroom instruction with online learning activities

words.reading: クリティカル・シンキング
words.jpTranslation: 批判的思考(ひはんてきしこう)
exampleSentences: Critical thinking helps readers evaluate whether news sources are trustworthy.
Debate club is one of the best activities for developing critical thinking in teenagers.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to analyze information carefully and form reasoned, independent judgments

words.reading: プロブレム・ソルビング
words.jpTranslation: 問題解決能力(もんだいかいけつのうりょく)
exampleSentences: Engineers rely on creative problem-solving when standard methods fail to work.
Group projects develop problem-solving skills by forcing students to handle disagreements.
words.englishDefinition: the process of finding effective solutions to difficult or complex challenges

words.jpTranslation: 仲間学習(なかまがくしゅう)
exampleSentences: Peer learning pairs stronger students with those who are struggling, benefiting both sides.
Through peer learning, classmates often explain concepts in ways the teacher cannot.
words.englishDefinition: a method where students teach and learn from each other rather than from a teacher

exampleSentences: The government increased funding for STEM programs to close the shortage of engineers.
Girls are still underrepresented in STEM fields despite progress over the past decade.
words.englishDefinition: the combined fields of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics in education

exampleSentences: A liberal arts education trains students to write persuasively, argue logically, and think broadly.
She chose a liberal arts college so she could study philosophy alongside data science.
words.englishDefinition: a broad academic education covering humanities, social sciences, arts, and natural sciences

exampleSentences: Vocational programs prepare students for careers in plumbing, nursing, or culinary arts.
He enrolled in a vocational college to become a licensed electrician in two years.
words.englishDefinition: relating to practical training for a specific trade or occupation

exampleSentences: Her summer internship at a law firm gave her real experience drafting contracts.
Many companies use internships to identify talented graduates before hiring them full-time.
words.englishDefinition: a temporary work placement where a student or trainee gains practical experience

words.reading: エクストラカリキュラー
words.jpTranslation: 課外活動の(かがいかつどうの)
exampleSentences: Universities look for applicants who have strong extracurricular involvement, not just high grades.

words.englishDefinition: describing activities done outside regular school lessons, such as sports or clubs

words.jpTranslation: 卒業生(そつぎょうせい)
exampleSentences: The university's alumni donated funds to build a new engineering research center.
She stays connected with fellow alumni through an annual reunion held on campus.
words.englishDefinition: people who have graduated from a particular school or university

exampleSentences: The faculty voted to change the grading policy during last month's staff meeting.
She was hired as a junior faculty member after completing her postdoctoral research.
words.englishDefinition: the teaching and academic staff of a university or college department

words.jpTranslation: 学籍係(がくせきがかり)
exampleSentences: Students must submit their course selection form to the registrar by Friday.
The registrar's office keeps official records of enrollment, grades, and transcripts.
words.englishDefinition: the university official responsible for student records, enrollment, and academic administration

words.reading: アクレディテーション
exampleSentences: The business school lost its accreditation after inspectors found serious curriculum flaws.
Employers often check whether a candidate's institution holds recognized accreditation.
words.englishDefinition: official recognition that a school or program meets established quality standards

words.reading: スタンダライズド・テスト
words.jpTranslation: 標準化テスト(ひょうじゅんかテスト)
exampleSentences: Critics argue that standardized tests favor students from wealthier backgrounds.
She prepared for the standardized test by practicing past papers every evening.
words.englishDefinition: an exam administered and scored in the same way for all test-takers to allow fair comparison

exampleSentences: Submitting another student's essay as your own is a clear case of plagiarism.
Turnitin software helps universities detect plagiarism by comparing submissions to online sources.
words.englishDefinition: the act of using someone else's words or ideas without credit, presenting them as your own

words.reading: ピア・フィードバック
words.jpTranslation: 相互評価(そうごひょうか)
exampleSentences: After presenting her essay, she received peer feedback that helped her reorganize her argument.
The teacher built peer feedback sessions into each writing unit so students could learn from each other.
words.englishDefinition: evaluation or comments given by classmates or equals rather than by a teacher

words.reading: ラーニング・オブジェクティブ
words.jpTranslation: 学習目標(がくしゅうもくひょう)
exampleSentences: The teacher wrote each learning objective on the board so students knew exactly what they were expected to master.
By the end of the unit, every learning objective had been covered through hands-on activities.
words.englishDefinition: a specific, stated goal describing what a student should be able to do after a lesson

exampleSentences: Spacing out study sessions dramatically improves long-term retention of new vocabulary.
Without regular review, retention drops sharply within a week of learning new material.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to keep information in memory over time

exampleSentences: Her comprehension of the passage improved once she looked up the unfamiliar words.
The quiz revealed gaps in students' comprehension of the chapter on photosynthesis.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to understand the meaning of what one reads, hears, or studies

words.reading: インクワイアリー・ベースド・ラーニング
words.jpTranslation: 探究学習(たんきゅうがくしゅう)
exampleSentences: Through inquiry-based learning, students designed their own experiments to test whether plants need sunlight.
The science department shifted to inquiry-based learning to encourage curiosity over memorization.
words.englishDefinition: an educational approach where students explore questions and problems to build their own understanding

words.reading: ディファレンシエイテッド・インストラクション
words.jpTranslation: 個別指導法(こべつしどうほう)
exampleSentences: Differentiated instruction allowed the teacher to give advanced readers longer texts while beginners used simpler ones.
With differentiated instruction, no student was left behind simply because they learned at a different pace.
words.englishDefinition: a teaching method that adjusts content, pace, or tasks to meet each student's individual needs

words.reading: スキャフォールディング
exampleSentences: The teacher provided sentence starters as scaffolding so students could attempt the essay with confidence.

words.englishDefinition: temporary support structures or guidance a teacher provides to help students complete tasks they cannot yet do alone

words.reading: アカデミック・インテグリティ
words.jpTranslation: 学術的誠実さ(がくじゅつてきせいじつさ)
exampleSentences: Submitting someone else's code as your own violates academic integrity and can lead to expulsion.

words.englishDefinition: the commitment to honest and ethical behavior in all academic work, including avoiding plagiarism and cheating

words.reading: ディスタンス・ラーニング
words.jpTranslation: 遠隔学習(えんかくがくしゅう)
exampleSentences: Distance learning allowed rural students to attend lectures from universities in the capital.
She completed her master's degree entirely through distance learning while working full-time.
words.englishDefinition: education delivered to students who are not physically present in a classroom, typically online

words.reading: スペシャル・エデュケーション
words.jpTranslation: 特別支援教育(とくべつしえんきょういく)
exampleSentences: The school hired two additional aides to support students enrolled in special education programs.
Special education teachers receive extra training to adapt lessons for children with diverse learning needs.
words.englishDefinition: tailored educational programs and services designed for students with disabilities or exceptional learning needs

words.jpTranslation: 才能ある(さいのうある)
exampleSentences: The gifted student finished the assignment in ten minutes and asked for a harder challenge.
A gifted artist, she had won regional competitions before she turned twelve.
words.englishDefinition: having exceptional natural ability or talent in one or more areas

words.reading: インクルーシブ・エデュケーション
words.jpTranslation: インクルーシブ教育(インクルーシブきょういく)
exampleSentences: Inclusive education places students with disabilities in mainstream classrooms alongside their peers.
Research shows that inclusive education benefits both disabled and non-disabled students socially and academically.
words.englishDefinition: an educational approach that welcomes all students, regardless of ability or background, into the same learning environment

exampleSentences: The presidential speech was broadcast live on every major television network.
The radio station broadcasts traffic updates every thirty minutes during rush hour.
words.englishDefinition: to transmit audio or video content to the public over radio, television, or the internet

exampleSentences: Investigative journalism uncovered the financial fraud that had been hidden from the public for years.
She chose journalism because she believed in the power of accurate reporting to hold governments accountable.
words.englishDefinition: the profession of gathering, verifying, and presenting news and information to the public

exampleSentences: The newspaper's editorial argued that the city council's budget cuts would harm public schools.
She wrote a sharp editorial criticizing the lack of transparency in the election process.
words.englishDefinition: an article in a newspaper or magazine that expresses the opinion of the editor or publication

words.reading: メディア・リテラシー
exampleSentences: Strong media literacy skills help people distinguish reliable news sources from sensationalist ones.
Schools that teach media literacy see students who are less likely to share unverified stories online.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to critically analyze, evaluate, and create media content

exampleSentences: The fake news story claiming the mayor had resigned spread across social media before journalists debunked it.
She verified the claim through three separate sources before concluding it was fake news.
words.englishDefinition: false or misleading information presented as legitimate news to deceive readers

exampleSentences: The skincare brand partnered with a popular influencer whose followers matched their target demographic.
Being a successful influencer requires consistent content, audience engagement, and a recognizable personal style.
words.englishDefinition: a person who builds a large online following and uses that audience to shape opinions or promote products

words.jpTranslation: 拡散する(かくさんする)
exampleSentences: The clip of the toddler singing opera went viral overnight, reaching forty million views by morning.
A viral post can boost a small business's visibility more than months of traditional advertising.
words.englishDefinition: describing content that spreads very rapidly to a huge number of people online

exampleSentences: The headline 'You Won't Believe What This Celebrity Did' turned out to be pure clickbait with no substance.
News sites that rely on clickbait sacrifice long-term credibility for short-term traffic.
words.englishDefinition: sensationalized or misleading headlines designed to lure people into clicking on a link

words.jpTranslation: 定期購読(ていきこうどく)
exampleSentences: Her monthly subscription to the digital newspaper gave her unlimited access to archived articles.
When the price rose, thousands of users canceled their subscriptions and switched to free alternatives.
words.englishDefinition: a regular payment made to receive a service or publication on an ongoing basis

exampleSentences: The post received low engagement because it was published at three in the morning when followers were asleep.

words.englishDefinition: the level of interaction — such as likes, comments, and shares — that an audience has with online content

words.reading: コンテント・クリエーター
words.jpTranslation: コンテンツクリエーター
exampleSentences: As a content creator, she films, edits, and uploads three videos each week to keep her channel active.
Many content creators earn income through brand sponsorships rather than platform advertising revenue alone.
words.englishDefinition: a person who produces original material — videos, articles, or images — for online audiences

exampleSentences: She listens to a history podcast during her commute and arrives at work feeling genuinely informed.
Launching a podcast requires only a decent microphone and a topic you can discuss in depth each week.
words.englishDefinition: a digital audio show made up of episodes that listeners can download or stream on demand

words.jpTranslation: ライブ配信(ライブはいしん)
exampleSentences: The band couldn't tour, so they gave a live stream concert watched by fans in thirty countries.
During the earthquake, local reporters used live stream on their phones to show the damage in real time.
words.englishDefinition: a real-time broadcast of video content transmitted over the internet as it happens

exampleSentences: The headline 'Record Heatwave Kills Hundreds in Europe' immediately grabbed readers' attention on the front page.
Editors choose a headline carefully because it determines whether anyone clicks on the article at all.
words.englishDefinition: the large title at the top of a news article that summarizes its main point

exampleSentences: She spent three weeks investigating before she felt confident enough to publish the article on data privacy.
The article in the science journal was peer-reviewed and cited over two hundred times within a year.
words.englishDefinition: a piece of writing on a specific topic published in a newspaper, magazine, or website

exampleSentences: His weekly column on personal finance attracted loyal readers who trusted his no-nonsense advice.
The opinion column sparked a heated debate in the letters section the following week.
words.englishDefinition: a regular feature in a newspaper or magazine written by the same author, usually expressing personal views or expertise

exampleSentences: The company issued a press release announcing the merger before the stock market opened.
Journalists received the press release by email and had two hours to prepare questions for the spokesperson.
words.englishDefinition: an official statement sent to news organizations to announce something newsworthy

exampleSentences: The journalist's interview with the refugee revealed details about the border crossing that no official report had mentioned.
She prepared twenty questions before the interview, but the conversation took unexpected and revealing turns.
words.englishDefinition: a formal conversation in which one person questions another to gather information for publication or broadcast

exampleSentences: The documentary followed three families living on the front lines of the climate crisis for an entire year.
After watching the documentary on ocean plastic, she gave up single-use bottles entirely.
words.englishDefinition: a non-fiction film or television program that presents factual information about real events or people

exampleSentences: The correspondent's live report from the disaster zone conveyed the scale of destruction more vividly than any official statement.
Inaccurate reports early in the crisis fueled public panic before corrections could be issued.
words.englishDefinition: a news account of an event or situation presented to the public through media

words.reading: バイアス・イン・メディア
exampleSentences: Readers noticed a clear bias in media coverage of the protest, with left-leaning outlets framing it very differently from conservative ones.
Teaching students to spot bias in media helps them evaluate which parts of a story may be incomplete or slanted.
words.englishDefinition: the tendency of news outlets to favor a particular perspective when selecting or presenting information

words.reading: ソース・クレディビリティ
words.jpTranslation: 情報源の信頼性(じょうほうげんのしんらいせい)
exampleSentences: Before sharing any article, she checks source credibility by tracing who originally reported the claim.

words.englishDefinition: the degree to which an information source can be trusted to provide accurate and reliable content

words.reading: インベスティゲイティブ・ジャーナリズム
words.jpTranslation: 調査報道(ちょうさほうどう)
exampleSentences: Months of investigative journalism exposed the corrupt contracting deals that had cost the city millions.

words.englishDefinition: in-depth reporting that uncovers hidden wrongdoing or important facts through extensive research and investigation

words.reading: フリーダム・オブ・プレス
words.jpTranslation: 報道の自由(ほうどうのじゆう)
exampleSentences: Reporters Without Borders ranked the country last in freedom of press after three journalists were jailed.

words.englishDefinition: the right of journalists and news organizations to report without government interference or censorship

words.reading: ミスインフォメーション
exampleSentences: Health officials struggled to counter misinformation about the vaccine that was spreading faster than accurate data.
Misinformation about the hurricane's path caused some residents to evacuate in the wrong direction.
words.englishDefinition: false or inaccurate information spread without necessarily intending to deceive

words.reading: ディスインフォメーション
exampleSentences: Intelligence agencies confirmed that the fabricated election story was coordinated disinformation from a foreign government.

words.englishDefinition: false information deliberately created and spread to deceive and manipulate people

exampleSentences: Living in an echo chamber online meant he only encountered opinions that already matched his own.
The debate team forced students to argue positions they disagreed with, breaking the echo chamber effect.
words.englishDefinition: an environment where a person only encounters beliefs and opinions that confirm their own, with no exposure to opposing views

exampleSentences: The platform's personalization trapped her in a filter bubble where climate skepticism never appeared in her feed.
Reading newspapers from different countries is one way to burst the filter bubble created by local algorithms.
words.englishDefinition: the state of intellectual isolation that results when algorithms show users only content matching their past behavior

words.reading: ネットワーク・テレビジョン
words.jpTranslation: 地上波テレビ(ちじょうはテレビ)
exampleSentences: Network television viewership has declined sharply as younger audiences shift to streaming platforms.
The presidential debate was broadcast simultaneously on all major network television channels to maximize reach.
words.englishDefinition: free-to-air television programming distributed nationally through a network of affiliated broadcast stations

exampleSentences: The drama's ratings collapsed after the lead actor left the show, dropping from eight to two million viewers.
Advertisers pay significantly more for slots during programs with high ratings during prime time.
words.englishDefinition: the measured percentage of an audience watching a particular television or radio program at a given time

exampleSentences: The advertisement showed children laughing while eating the cereal, deliberately targeting parents during Saturday cartoons.
She skipped every advertisement, so the brand paid for product placement within the show itself instead.
words.englishDefinition: a paid announcement or message designed to promote a product, service, or idea to an audience

words.reading: パブリック・リレーションズ
exampleSentences: After the data breach, the company hired a public relations firm to manage the fallout and rebuild trust.
Good public relations involves building genuine relationships with journalists, not just sending flattering press releases.
words.englishDefinition: the practice of managing how an organization communicates with the public to shape its image and reputation

exampleSentences: The late-night show used satire to mock the politician's contradictory statements, drawing laughter and pointed criticism.

words.englishDefinition: the use of humor, irony, or exaggeration to criticize and expose foolishness in people, politics, or society

words.jpTranslation: 検閲された(けんえつされた)
exampleSentences: The documentary was censored by authorities who removed every scene critical of the government's environmental record.

words.englishDefinition: having had content removed, blocked, or altered by an authority that deemed it unacceptable

words.jpTranslation: 発行部数(はっこうぶすう)
exampleSentences: The newspaper's print circulation fell from half a million to eighty thousand over the past decade.
High circulation gives a magazine more bargaining power when setting advertising rates.
words.englishDefinition: the total number of copies of a newspaper or magazine distributed or sold in a given period

words.reading: ブレーキング・ニュース
exampleSentences: The anchor interrupted the talk show with breaking news about a major earthquake off the coast.
In the age of social media, breaking news often appears on Twitter before television stations can confirm it.
words.englishDefinition: newly received information about an important or urgent event that is being reported immediately

exampleSentences: A retired general wrote an op-ed arguing that the defense budget should be redirected to veterans' healthcare.
The op-ed drew hundreds of letters from readers who disagreed strongly with the author's conclusions.
words.englishDefinition: a written opinion piece published in a newspaper or magazine by an outside contributor, not the editorial board

words.jpTranslation: 報道の自由(ほうどうのじゆう)
exampleSentences: The new law requiring journalists to reveal sources was condemned internationally as an attack on press freedom.
Countries with strong press freedom tend to rank higher in corruption indexes because wrongdoing gets exposed.
words.englishDefinition: the freedom of journalists and media to operate, investigate, and publish without government control or intimidation

words.jpTranslation: 編集室(へんしゅうしつ)
exampleSentences: The entire newsroom fell silent when the editor confirmed that the source had withdrawn their statement an hour before publication.
Layoffs at the local paper reduced the newsroom from thirty reporters to just seven.
words.englishDefinition: the space in a media organization where journalists gather, write, and edit news stories

words.jpTranslation: 署名記事(しょめいきじ)
exampleSentences: The byline read 'Reported from Kyiv by Maria Santos,' lending the article immediate geographic authority.
Some editors remove the byline from sensitive investigations to protect the reporter's safety.
words.englishDefinition: a line in a published article giving the name of the writer who reported or wrote it

exampleSentences: The reporter was careful to quote the minister's exact words to avoid any accusation of misrepresentation.
She quoted three independent scientists in the article to show the consensus on rising sea levels.
words.englishDefinition: to repeat or cite someone's exact words as evidence or support in speech or writing

words.jpTranslation: 言い換える(いいかえる)
exampleSentences: Instead of copying the statistic directly, she paraphrased it in simpler language for a general audience.
Good paraphrasing retains the original meaning while using entirely different wording and sentence structure.
words.englishDefinition: to restate someone else's ideas in your own words while keeping the original meaning

words.reading: メディア・アウトレット
words.jpTranslation: 報道機関(ほうどうきかん)
exampleSentences: Every major media outlet sent reporters to cover the trial, filling the courthouse with cameras and microphones.
A small independent media outlet broke the story weeks before the national networks picked it up.
distractors: 相互連結性(そうごれんけつせい)
相関関係(そうかんかんけい)
words.englishDefinition: a company or platform — such as a newspaper, TV channel, or website — that produces and distributes news and information

words.jpTranslation: タブロイド紙(タブロイドし)
exampleSentences: The tabloid ran a front-page story about the celebrity's divorce.
She refuses to read tabloids because they sensationalize minor scandals.
words.englishDefinition: a small-format newspaper that focuses on sensational or celebrity news

exampleSentences: He reads a broadsheet every morning to follow economic and political news.
The broadsheet devoted three pages to the prime minister's policy speech.
words.englishDefinition: a large-format, serious newspaper known for in-depth reporting on politics and current affairs

words.reading: プレス・コンファレンス
words.jpTranslation: 記者会見(きしゃかいけん)
exampleSentences: The mayor held a press conference to explain the city's flood response.
Reporters shouted questions at the athlete during the post-match press conference.
words.englishDefinition: a meeting at which a public figure answers questions from journalists

words.jpTranslation: 新聞のコラム幅(しんぶんのコラムはば)
exampleSentences: Advertisers pay a fixed rate per column inch in the weekend edition.
The story received only two column inches despite its significance.
words.englishDefinition: a unit of newspaper space one column wide and one inch tall, used to measure advertising or editorial content

exampleSentences: The company finally acknowledged that its product had a serious safety flaw.
He acknowledged his classmates' contributions before presenting the project.
words.englishDefinition: to admit or recognize that something is true or that someone has done something

exampleSentences: Engineers failed to anticipate how quickly the software would be hacked.
The store ordered extra stock because they anticipated high holiday demand.
words.englishDefinition: to expect something to happen and prepare for it in advance

words.jpTranslation: 補償する(ほしょうする)
exampleSentences: The airline compensated stranded passengers with hotel vouchers and meal tickets.
He worked overtime to compensate for the days he missed during illness.
words.englishDefinition: to give money or something else of value to make up for a loss or injury

words.jpTranslation: 強調する(きょうちょうする)
exampleSentences: The coach emphasized the importance of rest before the championship match.
Bold text is used to emphasize key terms in the textbook.
words.englishDefinition: to give special attention or importance to something

words.jpTranslation: 説明する(せつめいする)
exampleSentences: The speaker used a personal story to illustrate how poverty affects education.
This graph illustrates the sharp rise in smartphone ownership since 2010.
words.englishDefinition: to make something clearer by using examples, diagrams, or stories

exampleSentences: The school will implement a new anti-bullying policy starting next semester.

words.jpTranslation: 段階的な(だんかいてきな)
exampleSentences: There has been a gradual decline in the bee population over the past two decades.
Learning a language well requires gradual exposure rather than cramming everything at once.
words.englishDefinition: happening slowly and in small stages over time

words.jpTranslation: 避けられない(さけられない)
exampleSentences: Conflict between the two rival groups seemed inevitable after months of rising tension.
Technological disruption of traditional jobs is widely considered an inevitable consequence of progress.
words.englishDefinition: certain to happen and impossible to prevent

exampleSentences: There is an inherent tension between freedom of speech and the right not to be harmed by words.
Any financial investment carries inherent risk that cannot be completely eliminated.
words.englishDefinition: existing as a natural and permanent part of something

words.jpTranslation: 革新的な(かくしんてきな)
exampleSentences: The startup won an award for its innovative approach to reducing food waste in restaurants.
Her innovative use of recycled materials in sculpture attracted international attention.
words.englishDefinition: introducing new and creative ideas or methods that differ from existing ones

words.jpTranslation: 集中的な(しゅうちゅうてきな)
exampleSentences: She completed an intensive two-week Spanish course before her trip to Mexico.
Intensive farming methods produce high yields but can deplete soil nutrients quickly.
words.englishDefinition: involving a high level of effort, activity, or concentration within a short time

words.jpTranslation: 対話型の(たいわがたの)
exampleSentences: The museum installed interactive exhibits where visitors can conduct simple chemistry experiments.
Interactive online quizzes give students immediate feedback on their answers.
words.englishDefinition: designed for two-way communication or hands-on participation between people or a system

exampleSentences: A lasting friendship requires mutual trust built over time.
The two companies signed a mutual agreement to share research data.
words.englishDefinition: shared equally between two or more people or groups

words.jpTranslation: 否定的な(ひていてきな)
exampleSentences: Constant negative feedback from a manager can seriously damage an employee's confidence.
The clinical trial produced negative results, meaning the drug had no measurable effect.
words.englishDefinition: expressing denial, opposition, or the absence of a desired quality or outcome

exampleSentences: Switzerland remained neutral during both World Wars and did not join either side.
The mediator tried to stay neutral and listen carefully to both parties in the dispute.
words.englishDefinition: not supporting or favouring either side in a conflict or disagreement

words.jpTranslation: 継続中の(けいぞくちゅうの)
exampleSentences: The ongoing construction near the school has been disrupting morning classes for three weeks.
Researchers are monitoring the ongoing effects of the policy change on urban air quality.
words.englishDefinition: continuing to happen or exist at the present time without a clear end

exampleSentences: Scientists are testing which temperature is optimal for storing the new vaccine.
The coach adjusted the training schedule to find the optimal balance between rest and practice.
words.englishDefinition: the best or most favourable possible under given conditions

words.jpTranslation: 並行した(へいこうした)
exampleSentences: Two parallel investigations were launched simultaneously by different government agencies.
The novel follows two parallel storylines set fifty years apart.
words.englishDefinition: occurring at the same time or alongside something else in a corresponding way

words.jpTranslation: 受動的な(じゅどうてきな)
exampleSentences: Watching lectures without taking notes is a passive way to study that rarely leads to deep understanding.
He took a passive role in the discussion, nodding along but never voicing his own opinion.
words.englishDefinition: accepting what happens without taking action or showing resistance

words.jpTranslation: 永続的な(えいぞくてきな)
exampleSentences: Exposure to very loud music without ear protection can cause permanent hearing loss.
She was offered a permanent position at the company after six months as a temporary worker.
words.englishDefinition: lasting or intended to last indefinitely, without changing or ending

words.jpTranslation: 肯定的な(こうていてきな)
exampleSentences: Keeping a positive attitude during setbacks makes it easier to find practical solutions.
The experiment yielded positive results, confirming the researchers' original hypothesis.
words.englishDefinition: expressing agreement, optimism, or a favourable outcome

words.jpTranslation: 潜在的な(せんざいてきな)
exampleSentences: Scientists warn of the potential dangers of releasing genetically modified organisms into the wild.
The young pianist showed enormous potential, performing pieces far beyond her age level.
words.englishDefinition: existing as a possibility but not yet realized or confirmed

exampleSentences: The surgeon must make precise incisions measured in millimetres to avoid damaging nearby tissue.
Please give me a precise arrival time so I can arrange a pick-up.
words.englishDefinition: exact and accurate, with no errors or unnecessary vagueness

words.jpTranslation: 生産的な(せいさんてきな)
exampleSentences: Turning off social media notifications made her mornings far more productive.
The two teams had a productive discussion that resulted in a concrete action plan.
words.englishDefinition: achieving a significant amount of useful work or positive results

words.jpTranslation: 進歩的な(しんぽてきな)
exampleSentences: The new education minister introduced progressive policies to reduce inequality between rich and poor schools.
A progressive tax system charges higher earners a larger percentage of their income.
words.englishDefinition: favouring gradual social reform and the adoption of new ideas over traditional ones

exampleSentences: Losing a parent at a young age can have a profound influence on a child's emotional development.
The documentary raised profound questions about the ethics of artificial intelligence.
words.englishDefinition: having great depth, seriousness, or far-reaching impact

words.jpTranslation: 注目すべき(ちゅうもくすべき)
exampleSentences: The patient made a remarkable recovery and was walking again within three weeks of surgery.
It is remarkable that the ancient aqueduct has survived for more than two thousand years.
words.englishDefinition: worthy of attention because of being unusual, impressive, or extraordinary

words.jpTranslation: 硬直した(こうちょくした)
exampleSentences: The school's rigid dress code did not allow any variation even on special occasions.
A rigid negotiating position often prevents both sides from reaching a workable compromise.
words.englishDefinition: not willing to change; fixed and inflexible in structure or attitude

exampleSentences: The security team built a robust firewall to protect customer data from cyberattacks.
Researchers need robust evidence from multiple studies before recommending a new treatment.
words.englishDefinition: strong, well-built, and able to function effectively under demanding conditions

words.jpTranslation: 安定した(あんていした)
exampleSentences: The country's stable political environment attracts considerable foreign investment.
After adjusting the medication, the patient's condition became stable and her symptoms eased.
words.englishDefinition: not likely to change, fall, or become worse; firmly established

words.jpTranslation: 戦略的な(せんりゃくてきな)
exampleSentences: The company made a strategic decision to exit the smartphone market and focus on software.
Placing the new store near a train station was a strategic move to attract commuters.
words.englishDefinition: carefully planned to achieve a long-term goal or competitive advantage

words.jpTranslation: 主観的な(しゅかんてきな)
exampleSentences: Film reviews are subjective, so two critics can disagree sharply about the same movie.
Beauty standards are highly subjective and differ widely across cultures and time periods.
words.englishDefinition: based on personal feelings or opinions rather than verifiable facts

exampleSentences: The charity received a substantial donation that allowed it to open three new clinics.
Switching to public transport could lead to a substantial reduction in household carbon emissions.
words.englishDefinition: large in size, value, or importance; more than just a little

exampleSentences: The government promised to be transparent about how relief funds were allocated after the disaster.
A transparent hiring process publishes the selection criteria so all candidates know how they are judged.
words.englishDefinition: open and honest, with no hidden information or secret intentions

exampleSentences: Arguments over trivial matters like seating arrangements were wasting the team's valuable meeting time.
What seems trivial to an adult, such as a broken toy, can feel devastating to a young child.
words.englishDefinition: of little importance or value; not worth serious attention

words.jpTranslation: 典型的な(てんけいてきな)
exampleSentences: A typical school day in Japan begins with a morning assembly and cleaning duties.
Her reaction was typical of someone who had never been criticised in public before.
words.englishDefinition: having the usual qualities or characteristics that represent a particular type or group

words.jpTranslation: 根底にある(こんていにある)
exampleSentences: Doctors discovered that the underlying cause of his fatigue was an undiagnosed thyroid condition.
To solve a social problem effectively, you must address the underlying inequalities that create it.
words.englishDefinition: existing beneath the surface and forming the real basis or cause of something

exampleSentences: Each person's fingerprints are unique, making them reliable identifiers in forensic investigation.
The city's unique blend of ancient temples and modern skyscrapers draws millions of tourists annually.
words.englishDefinition: being the only one of its kind; unlike anything else

words.jpTranslation: 普遍的な(ふへんてきな)
exampleSentences: Music has a universal appeal that crosses language barriers.
The desire to belong is a universal human need.
words.englishDefinition: applying to or shared by all people or things without exception

exampleSentences: The doctor sent an urgent message asking her to return to the clinic immediately.
Fixing the leaking pipe became urgent after the ceiling started to crack.
words.englishDefinition: requiring immediate attention or action

words.jpTranslation: 目に見える(めにみえる)
exampleSentences: The scar on his chin was clearly visible in the photograph.
Smoke from the factory was visible from several kilometers away.
words.englishDefinition: able to be seen with the eyes

exampleSentences: Sleep is vital for the brain to process and store new information.
The goalkeeper played a vital role in stopping three penalty kicks.
words.englishDefinition: absolutely necessary for something to function or survive

exampleSentences: Misinformation about the vaccine spread widespread panic across social media.
Widespread flooding forced thousands of residents to evacuate their homes.
words.englishDefinition: existing or happening over a large area or among many people

exampleSentences: The contract's wording was so ambiguous that both sides interpreted it differently.
His ambiguous smile left her unsure whether he was joking or serious.
words.englishDefinition: having more than one possible meaning and therefore unclear

words.jpTranslation: 野心的な(やしんてきな)
exampleSentences: She set an ambitious target of running a marathon within six months of starting training.
The city announced an ambitious plan to plant a million trees by 2030.
words.englishDefinition: having a strong desire to achieve difficult or important goals

exampleSentences: The whistleblower chose to remain anonymous to avoid losing their job.
An anonymous donor gave the school enough money to build a new library.
words.englishDefinition: not identified by name; having an unknown or withheld identity

exampleSentences: It was apparent from her tone that she disagreed with the decision.
The apparent simplicity of the puzzle hid a surprisingly tricky solution.
words.englishDefinition: clearly seen or understood; obvious to the eye or mind

exampleSentences: The teacher's grading felt arbitrary because two similar essays received very different marks.
Borders drawn by colonial powers were often arbitrary, splitting ethnic communities in two.
words.englishDefinition: based on personal whim rather than reason or a consistent rule

exampleSentences: The restaurant uses authentic recipes passed down through three generations of the owner's family.
Experts confirmed that the signature on the painting was authentic.
words.englishDefinition: genuinely what it is claimed to be; not copied or fake

exampleSentences: Regular outdoor walks proved beneficial for patients recovering from depression.
The trade agreement was beneficial to farmers on both sides of the border.
words.englishDefinition: having a good or helpful effect on someone or something

exampleSentences: The engineer was capable of solving problems that stumped her entire team.
With the right training, most people are capable of running a 5K race.
words.englishDefinition: having the ability or skill needed to do something

words.jpTranslation: 一貫した(いっかんした)
exampleSentences: After rewriting the introduction, her essay finally had a coherent structure.
He was so nervous during the interview that he struggled to give a coherent answer.
words.englishDefinition: logically consistent and clearly organized so that ideas fit together

exampleSentences: The festival ran three concurrent stages so attendees could choose different performers.
He served two concurrent sentences, meaning his total prison time was four years, not eight.
words.englishDefinition: happening or existing at the same time

words.jpTranslation: 決断力のある(けつだんりょくのある)
exampleSentences: The coach made a decisive substitution in the final minute that led to the winning goal.
She impressed the committee with her decisive response to the budget crisis.
words.englishDefinition: able to make decisions quickly and confidently; settling a matter conclusively

exampleSentences: Many teenagers are deficient in vitamin D because they spend most of their time indoors.
The report found the company's safety procedures deficient in several key areas.
words.englishDefinition: lacking enough of something necessary; falling short of a required standard

exampleSentences: His deliberate mispronunciation of her name was clearly meant to annoy her.
Choosing a smaller portion was a deliberate step toward a healthier diet.
words.englishDefinition: done on purpose and after careful consideration, not by accident

words.jpTranslation: 効率的な(こうりつてきな)
exampleSentences: The new scheduling software made the delivery routes far more efficient.
She reorganized the kitchen so that cooking dinner became a much more efficient process.
words.englishDefinition: achieving results without wasting time, energy, or resources

exampleSentences: One cup of spinach provides calcium roughly equivalent to half a glass of milk.
A British A-level qualification is broadly equivalent to the first year of a US university.
words.englishDefinition: equal in value, amount, meaning, or effect

words.jpTranslation: 倫理的な(りんりてきな)
exampleSentences: The journalist faced an ethical dilemma about whether to publish the private information.
Consumers increasingly prefer to buy from companies with ethical supply chains.
words.englishDefinition: relating to moral principles about what is right and wrong

exampleSentences: It was evident from the empty seats that the event had attracted fewer visitors than expected.
Her passion for the subject was evident in the detailed notes she kept.
words.englishDefinition: plain and obvious; clearly seen or understood

words.jpTranslation: 実行可能な(じっこうかのうな)
exampleSentences: Engineers assessed whether building a bridge across the gorge was technically feasible.
A four-day workweek sounds appealing, but is it financially feasible for small businesses?
words.englishDefinition: possible to do or achieve within existing conditions and resources

words.jpTranslation: 世界的な(せかいてきな)
exampleSentences: Climate change is a global challenge that no single country can solve alone.
The pandemic disrupted global supply chains and caused shortages of basic goods.
words.englishDefinition: relating to or affecting the entire world

exampleSentences: The two fingerprints were not identical, so the suspect was cleared of the charge.
Both factories produced identical components, allowing either one to supply the assembly line.
words.englishDefinition: exactly the same in every detail

exampleSentences: The fire alarm triggered an immediate evacuation of all three floors.
He sent an immediate reply to the job offer before another candidate could respond.
words.englishDefinition: happening or done at once, without any delay

words.jpTranslation: 暗示的な(あんじてきな)
exampleSentences: There was an implicit understanding between the teammates that no one would discuss the loss publicly.
The advertisement carried an implicit message that buying the product would make you more popular.
words.englishDefinition: suggested or understood without being directly stated

words.jpTranslation: 段階的な(だんかいてきな)
exampleSentences: The software received incremental updates each month rather than one large annual release.
Her confidence grew through incremental achievements, each one building on the last.
words.englishDefinition: happening gradually through small, successive additions or improvements

words.jpTranslation: 独立した(どくりつした)
exampleSentences: An independent review board investigated the complaint to avoid any conflict of interest.
She became financially independent after landing her first full-time job.
words.englishDefinition: not relying on or controlled by others; free to think or act for oneself

words.jpTranslation: 影響力のある(えいきょうりょくのある)
exampleSentences: Rachel Carson's book 'Silent Spring' was one of the most influential environmental texts of the 20th century.
He became influential in local politics by organizing community clean-up campaigns.
words.englishDefinition: having the power to shape opinions, decisions, or events

words.jpTranslation: 統合された(とうごうされた)
exampleSentences: The app has an integrated calendar so users can schedule tasks without switching programs.
The school runs an integrated curriculum that combines science, math, and art in each unit.
words.englishDefinition: combining different parts into a unified whole that works together smoothly

words.jpTranslation: 意味のある(いみのある)
exampleSentences: Volunteering at the shelter gave her a sense of meaningful contribution to her community.
He found it hard to maintain meaningful friendships after moving to a new country.
words.englishDefinition: having real significance, purpose, or value beyond the surface level

exampleSentences: Doctors recommend moderate aerobic exercise, such as brisk walking, for at least 150 minutes per week.
The committee adopted a moderate position, accepting some reforms while rejecting the most radical ones.
words.englishDefinition: neither very large nor very small; avoiding extremes

exampleSentences: She applied to multiple universities to improve her chances of acceptance.
The blackout had multiple causes, including aging infrastructure and unusually high demand.
words.englishDefinition: consisting of more than one; many

exampleSentences: It was obvious that the bridge needed repairs after cracks appeared along the support beams.
Her disappointment was obvious when her name was not called as the winner.
words.englishDefinition: easy to see or understand; completely clear

words.jpTranslation: 根気強い(こんきづよい)
exampleSentences: Despite repeated rejections, he remained persistent and eventually found a publisher for his novel.
A persistent cough that lasts more than three weeks should be checked by a doctor.
words.englishDefinition: continuing firmly despite difficulties or opposition over a long period

exampleSentences: Preliminary data suggests the new drug reduces symptoms, but larger trials are still needed.
The teams met for a preliminary discussion before drafting the formal agreement.
words.englishDefinition: coming before the main event or action as an introductory or preparatory step

exampleSentences: A prominent activist gave a speech calling for stricter environmental regulations.
The café occupies a prominent corner location that attracts walk-in customers all day.
words.englishDefinition: well-known and important, or standing out so as to be easily noticed

words.jpTranslation: 理性的な(りせいてきな)
exampleSentences: When emotions run high, it can be difficult to make a rational decision.
A rational approach to saving money starts with tracking every expense for one month.
words.englishDefinition: based on clear thinking and reason rather than emotion or impulse

words.jpTranslation: 現実的な(げんじつてきな)
exampleSentences: Setting a realistic budget prevents disappointment when unexpected expenses arise.
The director pushed for a more realistic portrayal of working-class life in the film.
words.englishDefinition: having a sensible, accurate understanding of what is actually possible

words.jpTranslation: 信頼できる(しんらいできる)
exampleSentences: Passengers need a reliable train service that runs on time even in bad weather.
Always cite reliable sources so your readers can verify the claims you make.
words.englishDefinition: consistently dependable and able to be trusted to do what is expected

words.jpTranslation: 繰り返しの(くりかえしの)
exampleSentences: Factory workers often develop joint problems from repetitive motions performed thousands of times daily.
She found the data-entry job repetitive and started looking for work that offered more variety.
words.englishDefinition: involving the same actions or elements repeated many times, often to the point of monotony

words.jpTranslation: 徹底的な(てっていてきな)
exampleSentences: The mechanic did a thorough inspection and found three problems the previous garage had missed.
Thorough preparation for the debate gave her the confidence to answer unexpected questions.
words.englishDefinition: complete and careful, covering every part without leaving anything out

exampleSentences: A tolerant classroom encourages students to share different viewpoints without fear of mockery.
He tried to be tolerant of his roommate's habits, even when they disrupted his sleep.
words.englishDefinition: willing to accept beliefs, behaviors, or people that differ from one's own without judgment

words.jpTranslation: 伝統的な(でんとうてきな)
exampleSentences: The village still holds a traditional harvest festival every October with music and dancing.
Traditional paper maps are sometimes more reliable than GPS in areas with poor signal.
words.englishDefinition: following long-established customs or methods passed down through generations

words.reading: アンプレシデンテッド
words.jpTranslation: 前例のない(ぜんれいのない)
exampleSentences: The city recorded an unprecedented heatwave, with temperatures 12 degrees above the historical average.
Remote work on this scale was unprecedented, forcing companies to rethink office culture entirely.
words.englishDefinition: never having happened or existed before

exampleSentences: Nutritionists recommend a varied diet that includes different food groups every day.
The responses to the survey were varied, ranging from strong approval to outright opposition.
words.englishDefinition: showing a range of different types or forms; not all the same

words.jpTranslation: 自発的な(じはつてきな)
exampleSentences: Participation in the after-school program is voluntary, so students can opt out at any time.
She spent her weekends doing voluntary work at a local food bank.
words.englishDefinition: done willingly by choice and without being paid or forced

words.jpTranslation: 価値のある(かちのある)
exampleSentences: Learning a second language takes years of effort, but most people find it worthwhile.
The long hike up the mountain was worthwhile because the view from the top was spectacular.
words.englishDefinition: worth the time, money, or effort spent on it

words.reading: アプロキシメットリー
exampleSentences: The flight from Tokyo to London takes approximately twelve hours.
Approximately 40% of respondents said they exercise fewer than twice a week.
words.englishDefinition: close to a particular number or amount but not exactly

exampleSentences: Traffic on the highway improved considerably after the new bypass opened.
Her test scores rose considerably after she started studying with a tutor twice a week.
words.englishDefinition: to a noticeably large degree; much more or much less than before

words.jpTranslation: 効果的に(こうかてきに)
exampleSentences: Using visuals helps teachers explain complex grammar points more effectively.
The new policy effectively doubled the fines for illegal dumping.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that produces the intended result successfully

words.reading: シグニフィカントリー
exampleSentences: Air pollution has decreased significantly since stricter car emission laws were introduced.
Her grades improved significantly after she joined the after-school study group.
words.englishDefinition: by a large and noticeable amount

exampleSentences: The factory closed down; consequently, hundreds of workers lost their jobs.
She skipped breakfast every morning and consequently felt tired by noon.
words.englishDefinition: as a direct result of something that happened before

words.jpTranslation: 根本的に(こんぽんてきに)
exampleSentences: The new manager's approach to teamwork was fundamentally different from her predecessor's.
Remote work has fundamentally changed how many companies think about office space.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that relates to the most basic nature of something

exampleSentences: The patient gradually regained strength over the three months after surgery.
As he spent more time abroad, his accent gradually shifted.
words.englishDefinition: slowly and in small stages over time

words.reading: インクリーシングリー
exampleSentences: Young voters are increasingly concerned about climate policy.
Doctors find it increasingly difficult to schedule enough time with each patient.
words.englishDefinition: more and more as time goes on

words.jpTranslation: 本質的に(ほんしつてきに)
exampleSentences: Competitive sports are not inherently dangerous if players follow the safety rules.
Some philosophers argue that humans are inherently social creatures who need community.
words.englishDefinition: as a permanent, built-in quality that cannot be separated from something

exampleSentences: The townspeople initially opposed the new wind farm, but changed their minds after the public meeting.
She was initially assigned to the marketing team before moving to product development.
words.englishDefinition: at the beginning, before anything else happened

exampleSentences: The campaign's success was largely due to its strong social media presence.
The ancient forest has remained largely untouched for over five centuries.
words.englishDefinition: to a great extent; mostly

exampleSentences: He is merely a part-time volunteer, not an official spokesperson for the organization.
The councillor's statement was merely a summary of what the committee had already agreed on.
words.englishDefinition: only; nothing more than what is stated

exampleSentences: The new bridge will cut travel time; moreover, it will create three hundred construction jobs.
She speaks Japanese fluently; moreover, she has passed the highest level of the JLPT.
words.englishDefinition: in addition to what has just been said; also and more importantly

exampleSentences: The hike was exhausting; nonetheless, everyone agreed it was worth the effort.
Evidence against the theory is growing; it remains popular nonetheless.
words.englishDefinition: despite what was just mentioned; still

exampleSentences: Several countries, notably Germany and Denmark, have invested heavily in offshore wind energy.
The album was notably different from her earlier work, with a jazz-influenced sound.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that is particularly worth mentioning

exampleSentences: The lights are off, so the office is presumably closed for the holiday.
He left without saying goodbye, presumably because he was running late for his train.
words.englishDefinition: used to say that something is likely true based on reasonable assumptions

exampleSentences: The app was designed primarily for students who need help organizing their homework.
The nonprofit focuses primarily on providing clean water to rural communities.
words.englishDefinition: mainly; above all other things

exampleSentences: Without treatment, the condition grows progressively worse over several years.
The course material becomes progressively more challenging each semester.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that happens gradually but steadily, step by step

exampleSentences: Wolves are rarely spotted in this region since the population declined in the 1980s.
He rarely checks his email on weekends, so contact him by phone for urgent matters.
words.englishDefinition: not very often; almost never

words.jpTranslation: 比較的に(ひかくてきに)
exampleSentences: Compared to living in Tokyo, the cost of renting in Sendai is relatively low.
The surgery is relatively straightforward and usually takes under an hour.
words.englishDefinition: to a moderate degree when compared with others

words.jpTranslation: 具体的に(ぐたいてきに)
exampleSentences: The guidelines were written specifically to address the needs of adult learners returning to school.
She asked the chef to specifically avoid any dish containing nuts due to her allergy.
words.englishDefinition: in a precise and particular way; referring to one exact thing

exampleSentences: The company filed for bankruptcy and subsequently laid off all of its staff.
The virus was first detected in livestock and subsequently spread to humans.
words.englishDefinition: happening after something else; afterward

exampleSentences: The renovation substantially increased the building's energy efficiency.
Her performance in the second semester was substantially better than in the first.
words.englishDefinition: to a large and significant degree

words.jpTranslation: 徹底的に(てっていてきに)
exampleSentences: The inspector thoroughly examined every room before issuing the safety certificate.
Read the contract thoroughly before signing, especially the cancellation clause.
words.englishDefinition: in a complete and careful way, missing nothing

words.jpTranslation: 典型的に(てんけいてきに)
exampleSentences: Job interviews in this field typically include a technical test lasting about one hour.
Teenagers typically need between eight and ten hours of sleep per night.
words.englishDefinition: in the way that is usual or most common for something

words.jpTranslation: 最終的に(さいしゅうてきに)
exampleSentences: After months of debate, the board ultimately decided to relocate the headquarters to Osaka.
Parents guide their children, but ultimately each person must choose their own path.
words.englishDefinition: in the end, after everything else has been considered or done

words.reading: アンフォチュネットリー
exampleSentences: Unfortunately, the outdoor concert was cancelled due to the approaching typhoon.
He studied hard for months; unfortunately, he fell ill on exam day.
words.englishDefinition: used to say that something is sad or disappointing

exampleSentences: With this software update, the old bug has been virtually eliminated from the system.
Virtually every student in the class passed the final exam with flying colors.
words.englishDefinition: almost completely; in all important respects

exampleSentences: The technique is now widely used in hospitals across Europe and North America.
Her research on sleep disorders is widely cited in medical journals.
words.englishDefinition: by or among many people or over a large area

exampleSentences: The retired teacher willingly tutored neighborhood kids every Saturday morning.
He willingly shared his notes with classmates who had missed the lecture.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that shows you are happy and ready to do something without being forced

exampleSentences: The evidence they found was absolutely crucial to winning the court case.
She was absolutely certain she had locked the door before leaving the house.
words.englishDefinition: completely and without any doubt or exception

exampleSentences: The weather forecast predicted heavy rain, so the organizers adjusted their outdoor plans accordingly.
Once the new regulations were announced, all departments updated their procedures accordingly.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that matches or suits the circumstances

exampleSentences: The report failed to adequately explain why the project went over budget.
Students must be adequately prepared before attempting the advanced certification exam.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that is good enough to meet what is needed

words.jpTranslation: 伝えられるところでは
exampleSentences: The executive allegedly approved payments that violated the company's ethics policy.
The substance found at the scene allegedly matched chemicals banned under international law.
words.englishDefinition: according to what people claim, but not yet proven

exampleSentences: You can submit your application by post; alternatively, you can upload the documents online.
Take the highway to save time; alternatively, the coastal road is more scenic.
words.englishDefinition: used to introduce a different choice or option

exampleSentences: Broadly speaking, the two political parties agree on environmental goals but differ on the methods.
The new curriculum is broadly similar to the previous one, with a few updated units.
words.englishDefinition: in a general way, without focusing on specific details

words.jpTranslation: 注意深く(ちゅういぶかく)
exampleSentences: She carefully reviewed each paragraph of the essay before submitting it.
Carefully consider the long-term consequences before making a major financial commitment.
words.englishDefinition: with close attention and caution to avoid mistakes or harm

exampleSentences: The teacher clearly explained the difference between a simile and a metaphor.
His frustration was clearly visible during the press conference.
words.englishDefinition: in a way that is easy to understand or impossible to miss

words.reading: コンティニュアスリー
words.jpTranslation: 継続的に(けいぞくてきに)
exampleSentences: The monitoring system runs continuously, recording data every few seconds.
She has been continuously updating the database for the past three hours.
words.englishDefinition: without stopping or any break

exampleSentences: The government is currently reviewing proposals to lower the voting age to sixteen.
That model is currently out of stock, but more units arrive next week.
words.englishDefinition: at the present time, as things stand now

exampleSentences: The journalist deliberately omitted key facts to make the story more sensational.
She deliberately chose a quieter neighborhood to reduce her daily stress.

words.reading: フォー・インスタンス
exampleSentences: Several countries, for instance Denmark and Sweden, have achieved near-universal literacy.
There are many ways to reduce stress — for instance, regular exercise has been shown to help significantly.
words.englishDefinition: as one example among several possible ones

words.reading: イン・アザー・ワーズ
exampleSentences: The treatment has a very low success rate — in other words, most patients do not recover fully.
She said she needed more time; in other words, she was not ready to make a decision.
words.englishDefinition: used to express the same idea more clearly or simply

exampleSentences: To sum up, the evidence strongly suggests that diet affects long-term mental health.
To sum up the debate: both sides agree on the problem but disagree on the solution.
words.englishDefinition: used to briefly restate the main points of what has been discussed or argued

words.reading: オン・ザ・コントラリー
exampleSentences: Many assumed the policy would hurt businesses; on the contrary, most companies reported higher profits.
He didn't seem tired after the marathon — on the contrary, he was energetic and talkative.
words.englishDefinition: used to state the opposite of what has just been said or assumed

words.reading: アット・ファースト・グランス
exampleSentences: At first glance, the two statistical reports appear identical, but closer inspection reveals key differences.
At first glance, the proposal seems generous, but the conditions attached make it far less appealing.
words.englishDefinition: based on an initial, quick observation before examining something more carefully

words.reading: バイ・アンド・ラージ
exampleSentences: By and large, the new transportation system has reduced commute times across the city.
Critics found minor flaws, but by and large the film was praised for its originality.
words.englishDefinition: generally speaking, with only small exceptions; mostly or usually

words.reading: イン・ザ・ミーンタイム
exampleSentences: The permanent repair will take two weeks; in the meantime, residents can use the temporary bridge.
Results will not arrive until Friday — in the meantime, try not to worry too much.
words.englishDefinition: during the period of time between now and a future event, or between two events

words.reading: ソー・ファー・ソー・グッド
exampleSentences: "How is the experiment going?" "So far so good — all readings are within normal range."
We are halfway through the project and so far so good, with no major setbacks.
words.englishDefinition: everything has gone well up to this point, though there may be more challenges ahead

words.reading: オール・シングズ・コンシダード
exampleSentences: All things considered, the relief operation was a success despite the logistical challenges.
All things considered, moving abroad turned out to be the best decision she ever made.
words.englishDefinition: taking all the relevant facts and circumstances into account before forming a judgment

words.reading: バイ・オール・ミーンズ
exampleSentences: "May I borrow your dictionary?" "By all means — please help yourself."
If you feel the policy is unfair, by all means raise the issue at the next committee meeting.
words.englishDefinition: used to grant permission willingly or to strongly encourage someone to do something

words.reading: アップ・トゥ・デイト
exampleSentences: Make sure your vaccination records are up to date before travelling internationally.
The website's information on visa requirements is not up to date, so check the official embassy page.
words.englishDefinition: current, modern, or reflecting the most recent information or developments

words.reading: アウト・オブ・デイト
words.jpTranslation: 時代遅れの(じだいおくれの)
exampleSentences: The textbook's chapter on smartphones is completely out of date — it doesn't even mention apps.
Using out of date maps for navigation in a foreign city caused them to get lost twice.
words.englishDefinition: no longer current or useful; belonging to an earlier time and no longer relevant

words.reading: アズ・ア・マター・オブ・ファクト
exampleSentences: "Did you enjoy the concert?" "As a matter of fact, I left halfway through — it was too loud."
People assume Japan has few immigrants; as a matter of fact, over three million foreign residents live there.
words.englishDefinition: used to introduce a true statement, often one that corrects or surprises the listener

words.reading: イン・スパイト・オブ
exampleSentences: In spite of having no formal training, she became one of the country's most respected photographers.
The negotiations continued in spite of growing tensions between the two governments.
words.englishDefinition: without being prevented by a particular obstacle or negative factor; despite

exampleSentences: The charity provides meals regardless of a person's background or immigration status.
All employees must follow the safety guidelines regardless of their seniority.
words.englishDefinition: without being affected by or taking into account a particular factor

words.reading: ウィズ・リスペクト・トゥ
exampleSentences: With respect to academic performance, students in smaller classes scored significantly higher.
The report raises serious concerns with respect to the long-term sustainability of the pension system.
words.englishDefinition: concerning or in relation to a specific subject or area

words.reading: イン・アコーダンス・ウィズ
exampleSentences: All experiments were conducted in accordance with the university's ethical research guidelines.
Employees must dress in accordance with the company's formal dress code at client meetings.
words.englishDefinition: following or obeying a particular rule, law, standard, or instruction

words.reading: フォー・ザ・セイク・オブ
exampleSentences: The couple stayed together for the sake of their children, even though their relationship had broken down.
For the sake of clarity, let me explain the key terms before we begin the discussion.
words.englishDefinition: in order to benefit someone or achieve a particular purpose

words.reading: イン・ザ・イベント・ザット
exampleSentences: In the event that the flight is cancelled, passengers will receive a full refund within five business days.
Please save your work frequently in the event that the computer crashes unexpectedly.
words.englishDefinition: if a particular situation occurs; used to describe a contingency or possible future scenario

words.reading: アズ・オポーズド・トゥ
exampleSentences: The study focused on long-term lifestyle changes as opposed to short-term dieting.
Many students now prefer reading e-books as opposed to carrying heavy printed textbooks.
words.englishDefinition: in contrast with something; used to highlight a difference between two things

words.reading: イン・ザ・ウェイク・オブ
exampleSentences: New safety regulations were introduced in the wake of the industrial accident that killed twelve workers.
Public trust in social media companies declined sharply in the wake of the data breach scandal.
words.englishDefinition: following and as a consequence of a significant event, especially a negative one

words.reading: アズ・ファー・アズ・アイ・ノー
exampleSentences: As far as I know, no country has fully solved the problem of long-term nuclear waste storage.
The meeting has not been rescheduled, as far as I know.
words.englishDefinition: based on the information one currently has, though it may be incomplete

words.reading: アンダー・ザ・サーカムスタンシズ
exampleSentences: Given the damage caused by the flooding, extending the deadline was the right call under the circumstances.
Under the circumstances, the rescue team did remarkably well to reach the survivors so quickly.
words.englishDefinition: given the specific situation or conditions that exist at a particular time

words.reading: トゥ・ア・グレート・エクステント
