exampleSentences: A child's language development depends to a great extent on how much parents read to them.
To a great extent, the country's economic recovery was driven by its booming tech sector.
words.englishDefinition: largely or mostly, though not necessarily completely

words.reading: イン・ザ・ライト・オブ
exampleSentences: In the light of recent protests, the government agreed to review the controversial housing policy.
Doctors revised their treatment guidelines in the light of new clinical trial results.
words.englishDefinition: taking into account or considering particular facts or circumstances when making a judgment

words.reading: オン・ザ・グラウンズ・オブ
exampleSentences: The journalist's request for access was denied on the grounds of national security.
She challenged the dismissal on the grounds of discrimination against her cultural background.
words.englishDefinition: citing a specific reason or justification for an action or decision

exampleSentences: The situation in the region is far from stable, despite the recent ceasefire agreement.
Her first attempt at the recipe was far from successful, but she kept experimenting until she got it right.
words.englishDefinition: not at all; the opposite of what might be expected or hoped

words.reading: バイ・ミーンズ・オブ
exampleSentences: The scientists transmitted data from Mars by means of a deep-space communication network.
The organization raised awareness by means of a nationwide poster campaign.
words.englishDefinition: using a particular method, tool, or approach to achieve something

exampleSentences: You can borrow the car as long as you return it before midnight.
The peace agreement will hold as long as both sides respect the terms they signed.
words.englishDefinition: on the condition that; provided that a particular requirement is met

exampleSentences: You should drink at least eight glasses of water every day.
The concert lasted at least three hours, and the crowd loved every minute.
words.englishDefinition: not less than a specified amount or number; the minimum

exampleSentences: The old bridge is no longer safe to drive across.
He no longer feels nervous about speaking in front of large audiences.
words.englishDefinition: used to say that something that was true in the past has stopped being true

exampleSentences: She wrote the instructions clearly so that new employees could follow them easily.
He lowered his voice so that the sleeping baby wouldn't wake up.
words.englishDefinition: in order that a particular result or purpose may be achieved

exampleSentences: Even though the exam was difficult, most students finished on time.
She enjoyed the hike even though it rained for most of the afternoon.
words.englishDefinition: despite the fact that; used to show a contrast between two situations

words.reading: プロバイデッド・ザット
exampleSentences: You may borrow the car provided that you return it before midnight.
The school will fund the trip provided that enough students sign up.
words.englishDefinition: on the condition that; only if a certain requirement is met

exampleSentences: Tokyo is extremely crowded; in contrast, the rural towns nearby feel very quiet.
The second half of the film was exciting; in contrast, the opening scene dragged.
words.englishDefinition: used to highlight a clear difference between two things or situations

exampleSentences: She chose to walk to school rather than take the bus.
The manager preferred to discuss problems privately rather than in a group meeting.
words.englishDefinition: used to indicate that one thing is preferred or chosen over another

exampleSentences: The program teaches coding as well as critical thinking skills.
He speaks French as well as Spanish, which helps a lot at work.
words.englishDefinition: in addition to; along with something else

words.reading: イン・オーダー・トゥ
exampleSentences: She studied every evening in order to improve her test scores.
The company reduced packaging in order to cut down on plastic waste.
words.englishDefinition: with the purpose of achieving a specific goal or result

exampleSentences: Above all, a good leader must be honest with their team.
The design was stylish, but above all it had to be practical for daily use.
words.englishDefinition: more than anything else; most importantly

words.reading: ノット・オンリー・バット・オールソー
exampleSentences: The new app is not only free but also available in twelve languages.
She won not only the gold medal but also the award for best performance.
words.englishDefinition: used to emphasize that two things are both true, with the second adding extra force

exampleSentences: The flight was cancelled due to a severe thunderstorm over the city.
Several shops closed early due to a sudden power outage in the area.
words.englishDefinition: because of; as a result of a particular cause

exampleSentences: All passengers must check in prior to boarding the aircraft.
Prior to the interview, she spent a week researching the company thoroughly.
words.englishDefinition: before a particular time or event

words.reading: サブシクエント・トゥ
exampleSentences: Subsequent to the merger, hundreds of employees moved to the new headquarters.
The city rebuilt its flood defences subsequent to the disaster in 2019.
words.englishDefinition: coming after or following a particular event or time

words.reading: ウィズ・ア・ビュー・トゥ
exampleSentences: The government is reviewing tax laws with a view to reducing inequality.
She enrolled in an online course with a view to switching careers next year.
words.englishDefinition: with the intention or goal of doing something

words.reading: イン・コンジャンクション・ウィズ
exampleSentences: The police worked in conjunction with local schools to promote road safety.
The new drug is most effective in conjunction with regular physical exercise.
words.englishDefinition: together with; in combination with another person or thing

words.reading: ニードレス・トゥ・セイ
exampleSentences: Needless to say, arriving two hours late made a terrible first impression.
She had trained for months, so needless to say, she finished the race comfortably.
words.englishDefinition: used when something is so obvious it barely needs to be mentioned

words.reading: アズ・ア・コンシクエンス
exampleSentences: He missed every deadline, and as a consequence, lost the contract.
Forest fires increased as a consequence of the prolonged drought that summer.
words.englishDefinition: as a result of something that happened previously

words.reading: イン・レトロスペクト
exampleSentences: In retrospect, accepting that job offer was one of the best decisions she ever made.
In retrospect, the warning signs of the financial crisis were obvious.
words.englishDefinition: thinking about or judging a past event from the present, with the benefit of hindsight

words.reading: イット・ゴーズ・ウィズアウト・セイイング
exampleSentences: It goes without saying that you should cite your sources in an academic paper.
It goes without saying that wearing a helmet reduces the risk of head injuries.
words.englishDefinition: used to indicate that something is so obvious it does not need to be stated

words.reading: モア・オフン・ザン・ノット
exampleSentences: More often than not, the cheapest option ends up costing more in the long run.
When he cooks dinner, more often than not the kitchen ends up completely messy.
words.englishDefinition: happening in more than half of cases; usually

words.reading: ワンス・イン・ア・ワイル
exampleSentences: It's fine to eat junk food once in a while, as long as your diet is balanced overall.
Once in a while, she takes a long walk by the river just to clear her head.
words.englishDefinition: occasionally; sometimes but not regularly

words.reading: スーナー・オア・レイター
exampleSentences: If you keep skipping practice, sooner or later your performance will suffer.
The truth about what happened that night will come out sooner or later.
words.englishDefinition: at some point in the future, even if not immediately

words.reading: トゥ・メイク・マターズ・ワース
exampleSentences: The train was delayed for an hour, and to make matters worse, it started pouring rain.
He forgot his wallet at home, and to make matters worse, his phone battery died too.
words.englishDefinition: used to introduce an additional problem that makes a bad situation even worse

words.reading: オール・イン・オール
exampleSentences: All in all, the conference was well-organised and produced some useful outcomes.
There were a few rough patches, but all in all the trip was a great experience.
words.englishDefinition: considering everything together; taking all factors into account

words.reading: イン・ア・ナットシェル
exampleSentences: In a nutshell, the plan is to launch the product in three markets simultaneously.
In a nutshell, the study found that sleep deprivation seriously harms concentration.
words.englishDefinition: expressed very briefly; summarised in the fewest possible words

words.reading: ラスト・バット・ノット・リースト
words.jpTranslation: 最後に重要なこととして
exampleSentences: Last but not least, I'd like to thank everyone who volunteered their time this year.
Last but not least, the report highlighted the need for better mental health support.
words.englishDefinition: used before the final item in a list to stress that it is just as important as the others

exampleSentences: Now that the exams are over, she finally has time to read for pleasure.
Now that both sides have agreed, the peace talks can move forward productively.
words.englishDefinition: since something is now the case; given that a change has occurred

words.reading: オン・コンディション・ザット
exampleSentences: The landlord agreed to lower the rent on condition that the lease was extended.
She'll attend the conference on condition that her travel expenses are covered.
words.englishDefinition: only if a specific requirement or stipulation is satisfied

exampleSentences: Seeing that the roads were icy, they decided to cancel the drive and stay home.
Seeing that he had already read the report, the manager skipped the introduction.
words.englishDefinition: given that; because a particular fact or situation is apparent

words.reading: ウィズ・ザ・エイム・オブ
exampleSentences: The charity was founded with the aim of providing clean water to remote villages.
The team restructured its training schedule with the aim of peaking at the right moment.
words.englishDefinition: intending to achieve a specific goal or outcome

words.reading: フォー・ザ・タイム・ビーイング
exampleSentences: For the time being, staff will work from home until the office renovation is complete.
Let's keep the current plan for the time being and review it again next month.
words.englishDefinition: for now; during the present period, until something changes

words.reading: イン・ザ・コース・オブ
exampleSentences: In the course of her research, she discovered several previously unknown manuscripts.
Many unexpected friendships form in the course of a long journey.
words.englishDefinition: during the period of; as part of the process of something

words.jpTranslation: 実際には(じっさいには)
exampleSentences: The rule sounds fair, but in practice it places an unfair burden on smaller companies.
In practice, learning a second language requires daily exposure, not just classroom time.
words.englishDefinition: in reality; as things actually happen, rather than in theory

words.jpTranslation: 原則的には(げんそくてきには)
exampleSentences: In principle, everyone supports equal rights, but putting them into law is complicated.
The committee agreed in principle that funding should be increased next fiscal year.
words.englishDefinition: as a general idea or rule, even if the details have not yet been worked out

exampleSentences: On balance, the benefits of the new policy outweigh its costs to taxpayers.
On balance, it's wiser to save some money each month rather than spend everything.
words.englishDefinition: considering all the advantages and disadvantages together; overall

words.reading: トゥ・サム・エクステント
exampleSentences: Social media is to some extent responsible for increasing anxiety among teenagers.
To some extent, your diet affects how quickly you recover from illness.
words.englishDefinition: partly; up to a certain degree but not completely

words.reading: アンダー・コンシダレーション
words.jpTranslation: 検討中の(けんとうちゅうの)
exampleSentences: Several new bus routes are currently under consideration by the city council.
A proposal to extend school hours is under consideration, but no decision has been made.
words.englishDefinition: being actively thought about or examined before a decision is made

words.reading: ウィズアウト・エクセプション
words.jpTranslation: 例外なく(れいがいなく)
exampleSentences: All members of the team, without exception, are expected to attend the safety briefing.
The new curfew applies to everyone without exception, regardless of age or status.
words.englishDefinition: applying to every case or person, with no one or nothing left out

exampleSentences: Careful observation of the bird's behaviour revealed that it builds nests in spring.
The scientist recorded her observations in a notebook after each experiment.
words.englishDefinition: the act of watching something carefully to learn information about it

exampleSentences: The students designed an experiment to test whether plants grow faster with music.
His experiment confirmed that the new material conducts electricity at low temperatures.
words.englishDefinition: a scientific test carried out to discover or prove something

words.reading: データ・コレクション
words.jpTranslation: データ収集(データしゅうしゅう)
exampleSentences: Accurate data collection is the foundation of any reliable scientific study.
The team spent three months on data collection before analysing the survey results.
words.englishDefinition: the process of gathering and measuring information for research or analysis

words.jpTranslation: 研究室(けんきゅうしつ)
exampleSentences: The chemistry lab smelled strongly of vinegar after the students' acid experiments.
Researchers in the lab worked overnight to analyse the newly discovered bacteria.
words.englishDefinition: a room or building equipped for scientific experiments and research

exampleSentences: The museum has a rare dinosaur bone specimen on display in its main hall.
The biologist collected water specimens from the river to test for pollution levels.
words.englishDefinition: a sample of an organism or material collected for scientific study or display

exampleSentences: Under the microscope, the researcher could see individual blood cells clearly.
The student adjusted the microscope lens to get a sharper image of the leaf tissue.
words.englishDefinition: an instrument that magnifies very small objects so they can be studied closely

words.jpTranslation: 望遠鏡(ぼうえんきょう)
exampleSentences: With a powerful telescope, you can see the rings of Saturn on a clear night.
The observatory's new telescope can detect galaxies billions of light-years away.
words.englishDefinition: an optical instrument that makes distant objects, especially stars, appear nearer and larger

exampleSentences: Physics helped engineers calculate the exact force needed to launch the spacecraft.
She studied physics at university because she wanted to understand how the universe works.
words.englishDefinition: the branch of science concerned with the nature and properties of matter, energy, and forces

exampleSentences: In chemistry class, they learned why mixing baking soda and vinegar causes a fizzing reaction.
A background in chemistry is essential for developing new pharmaceutical drugs.
words.englishDefinition: the branch of science dealing with the composition, structure, and reactions of substances

exampleSentences: Biology class taught her how cells divide and copy their genetic information.
Marine biology is the study of living organisms that inhabit the ocean.
words.englishDefinition: the scientific study of living organisms, including their structure, function, and evolution

exampleSentences: Geology helped scientists understand why earthquakes occur along certain fault lines.
By studying rock layers, geologists can estimate how old the Earth's crust is in a region.
words.englishDefinition: the science that studies the Earth's physical structure, rocks, and the processes that shape them

exampleSentences: Astronomy revealed that the Milky Way contains hundreds of billions of stars.
She became fascinated with astronomy after watching a lunar eclipse as a child.
words.englishDefinition: the scientific study of stars, planets, and other objects in outer space

exampleSentences: Quantum mechanics describes the strange behaviour of particles at the subatomic level.
Scientists are using quantum principles to develop computers far faster than existing ones.
words.englishDefinition: relating to the smallest discrete unit of energy or matter in physics

exampleSentences: A water molecule consists of two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom.
When heated, the molecules in a gas move faster and spread further apart.
words.englishDefinition: the smallest unit of a chemical substance that retains its properties, made of bonded atoms

exampleSentences: Every atom has a nucleus made of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons.
Carbon atoms can bond in different ways, creating materials as different as diamond and graphite.
words.englishDefinition: the smallest particle of a chemical element that can exist and take part in a reaction

exampleSentences: Gold is a chemical element that does not rust or tarnish over time.
The periodic table organises all known elements by their atomic number and properties.
words.englishDefinition: a pure substance made of only one type of atom that cannot be broken down chemically

exampleSentences: Salt is a compound formed when sodium and chlorine atoms bond together.
Chemists synthesised a new compound that may be effective against drug-resistant bacteria.
words.englishDefinition: a substance formed when two or more different elements are chemically bonded together

exampleSentences: When iron is exposed to water and oxygen, a chemical reaction produces rust.
The unexpected reaction between the two chemicals released a large amount of heat.
words.englishDefinition: a chemical process in which substances interact to form one or more new substances

exampleSentences: Enzymes act as biological catalysts that speed up digestion without being consumed.
The discovery of penicillin served as a catalyst for the development of modern antibiotics.
words.englishDefinition: a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being permanently changed itself

exampleSentences: Scientists discovered that the particle had almost no mass at all.
Dust particles floating in the air can trigger allergies in sensitive people.
words.englishDefinition: an extremely small piece or unit of matter

exampleSentences: Workers at the nuclear plant wore special suits to protect themselves from radiation.
Doctors used targeted radiation to shrink the tumor without harming nearby tissue.
words.englishDefinition: energy emitted as waves or particles, especially from a radioactive source

exampleSentences: Without gravity, objects on the space station float freely around the cabin.
The apple falling from the tree helped Newton think about gravity as a universal force.
words.englishDefinition: the natural force that pulls objects toward each other, especially toward the Earth

exampleSentences: The bullet left the barrel at an extremely high velocity.
Scientists measured the velocity of the spacecraft as it passed Jupiter.
words.englishDefinition: the speed of something in a given direction

exampleSentences: The car's rapid acceleration pushed the passengers back into their seats.
A heavier object requires more force to achieve the same acceleration as a lighter one.
words.englishDefinition: the rate at which the speed or velocity of an object increases over time

exampleSentences: The mass of an object stays the same whether it is on Earth or the Moon.
Scientists calculated the mass of the asteroid before sending a probe to study it.
words.englishDefinition: the amount of matter contained in an object, which determines its resistance to acceleration

exampleSentences: Solar panels convert the sun's light directly into electrical energy.
When you brake a car, kinetic energy is converted into heat.
words.englishDefinition: the capacity to do work or cause physical change, existing in forms such as heat, light, or motion

exampleSentences: You need to apply more force to open the stuck drawer.
The force of the waves gradually eroded the base of the cliff.
words.englishDefinition: a push or pull that changes the motion or shape of an object

exampleSentences: The magnetic field around Earth protects us from harmful solar winds.
Iron filings arranged themselves in curved lines around the magnetic poles of the bar.
words.englishDefinition: relating to or having the properties of a magnet, attracting iron and other metals

exampleSentences: An electric current flows through the wire when the circuit is closed.
The lab measured the strength of the electric field between the two charged plates.
words.englishDefinition: relating to or produced by electricity

exampleSentences: The thermal camera revealed heat leaking from the poorly insulated roof.
Thermal energy from the hot spring is captured to generate electricity in Iceland.
words.englishDefinition: relating to heat or temperature

words.jpTranslation: 光学的な(こうがくてきな)
exampleSentences: The parallel lines create an optical illusion that makes them appear to curve.
Optical fiber cables carry data as pulses of light at incredible speeds.
words.englishDefinition: relating to light or the sense of sight

exampleSentences: A prism splits white light into the full visible spectrum of colors.
The panel members held views across the entire political spectrum.
words.englishDefinition: a continuous range of values, wavelengths, or ideas arranged from one extreme to another

exampleSentences: Dogs can hear sounds at a much higher frequency than humans can.
The radio station broadcasts at a frequency of 89.7 MHz.
words.englishDefinition: the number of complete wave cycles per second of a vibration, sound, or electromagnetic signal

exampleSentences: Red light has a longer wavelength than blue light.
The wavelength of the radio signal determines how far it can travel.
words.englishDefinition: the distance between two successive peaks of a wave, such as a sound or light wave

exampleSentences: Ice floats on water because its density is slightly lower than liquid water.
The high population density of Tokyo means millions commute on the same train lines.
words.englishDefinition: the degree to which something is closely packed or the mass of a substance per unit volume

exampleSentences: Air pressure drops as you climb higher above sea level.
The diver felt increasing pressure on her eardrums as she descended.
words.englishDefinition: the continuous physical force exerted on or against an object by something in contact with it

exampleSentences: The nurse checked the patient's temperature and found it was 38.5 degrees Celsius.
Bacteria multiply rapidly when the storage temperature rises above four degrees.
words.englishDefinition: a measure of how hot or cold something is, expressed in degrees

exampleSentences: Plug the numbers into the formula to calculate the area of the circle.
The chemistry teacher wrote the molecular formula for water on the board: H₂O.
words.englishDefinition: a mathematical or scientific rule expressed in symbols or letters

exampleSentences: Pythagoras's theorem states that the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the other two squares.
The student spent an hour trying to prove the theorem step by step.
words.englishDefinition: a statement in mathematics or logic that has been or can be proved from accepted principles

exampleSentences: She solved the quadratic equation by factoring both sides.
Einstein's famous equation E=mc² links energy directly to mass.
words.englishDefinition: a mathematical statement showing that two expressions are equal, often used to find an unknown value

exampleSentences: It takes Earth exactly 365 days to complete one orbit around the Sun.
The satellite was placed in a low orbit to capture detailed images of the surface.
words.englishDefinition: the curved path followed by a planet, moon, or satellite around a larger body in space

exampleSentences: Our solar system contains eight planets, all orbiting the same star.
Voyager 1 became the first spacecraft to leave the solar system and enter interstellar space.
words.englishDefinition: the collection of the Sun and all the planets, moons, and other bodies that orbit it

exampleSentences: The Milky Way galaxy contains hundreds of billions of stars.
Astronomers detected two galaxies colliding slowly over millions of years.
words.englishDefinition: a large system of billions of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity

exampleSentences: Darwin's theory of evolution explains how species change over many generations.
The evolution of whales from land mammals is supported by fossil evidence.
words.englishDefinition: the gradual process by which living things develop and change over time through natural selection

exampleSentences: Advances in genetics have made it possible to identify inherited diseases before birth.
She studied genetics to understand why certain traits run in families.
words.englishDefinition: the scientific study of genes and how traits are passed from parents to offspring

words.jpTranslation: 突然変異(とつぜんへんい)
exampleSentences: A random mutation gave the moth a darker wing color that helped it hide on soot-covered trees.
Some mutations in cancer cells allow the tumor to resist treatment.
words.englishDefinition: a change in the DNA sequence of an organism that can be inherited or triggered by environmental factors

words.reading: エコシステム・リサーチ
words.jpTranslation: 生態系研究(せいたいけいけんきゅう)
exampleSentences: Ecosystem research in the Amazon revealed that removing one predator disrupts the entire food chain.
Funding for ecosystem research has increased as governments prioritize biodiversity protection.
words.englishDefinition: the scientific study of how living organisms interact with each other and their environment

words.reading: サイエンティフィック・メソッド
words.jpTranslation: 科学的方法(かがくてきほうほう)
exampleSentences: The scientific method requires forming a hypothesis before running any experiment.
By following the scientific method, researchers ruled out several possible causes of the outbreak.
words.englishDefinition: a systematic process of observing, hypothesizing, experimenting, and analyzing to investigate questions

words.reading: コントロール・グループ
words.jpTranslation: 対照群(たいしょうぐん)
exampleSentences: The control group took a sugar pill while the other group received the actual drug.
Without a control group, researchers cannot tell whether the treatment caused the improvement.
words.englishDefinition: a group in an experiment that does not receive the treatment, used as a baseline for comparison

exampleSentences: Some patients in the trial improved even though they had only taken a placebo.
The placebo effect shows how strongly the mind can influence physical symptoms.
words.englishDefinition: an inactive substance given to some participants in a trial while others receive the real treatment

words.jpTranslation: 二重盲検の(にじゅうもうけんの)
exampleSentences: In a double-blind trial, neither the doctors nor the patients know who received the real drug.
Double-blind studies reduce the chance that expectations will skew the results.
words.englishDefinition: describing a study in which neither participants nor researchers know who receives the treatment or placebo

words.jpTranslation: 再現する(さいげんする)
exampleSentences: Other labs could not replicate the original findings, so scientists questioned the results.
The team replicated the experiment three times to confirm the data was consistent.
words.englishDefinition: to repeat an experiment or procedure to verify that the same results can be obtained

words.jpTranslation: 相関関係(そうかんかんけい)
exampleSentences: Researchers found a strong correlation between sleep deprivation and poor academic performance.
There is a clear correlation between daily exercise and lower rates of heart disease.
words.englishDefinition: a mutual relationship or statistical link between two variables that tend to change together

words.jpTranslation: 因果関係(いんがかんけい)
exampleSentences: The study showed correlation but could not prove causation between diet and the disease.
Establishing causation requires ruling out every other possible explanation for the result.
words.englishDefinition: the relationship in which one event or condition directly produces another as its result

exampleSentences: A sample size of only ten people is too small to draw reliable conclusions.
Increasing the sample size reduced the margin of error significantly in the survey.
words.englishDefinition: the number of individuals or observations included in a study or experiment

words.jpTranslation: 統計的な(とうけいてきな)
exampleSentences: The difference between the two groups was not statistical significance, so the result may be chance.
She used a statistical model to predict how many people would develop the illness.
words.englishDefinition: relating to the collection, analysis, and interpretation of numerical data

words.reading: クォンティテイティブ
words.jpTranslation: 定量的な(ていりょうてきな)
exampleSentences: The quantitative data showed that test scores improved by an average of 15 points.
Researchers used a quantitative approach, counting the exact number of species at each site.
words.englishDefinition: relating to or measured by quantity, involving numerical or measurable data

words.jpTranslation: 定性的な(ていせいてきな)
exampleSentences: The qualitative interviews revealed how students felt emotionally about remote learning.
Her qualitative analysis focused on the themes that appeared repeatedly in the responses.
words.englishDefinition: relating to the nature or quality of something rather than its quantity, often involving non-numerical data

words.jpTranslation: 再現性のある(さいげんせいのある)
exampleSentences: A finding is only accepted in science when it is reproducible by independent teams.
The lab shared its full procedure so others could check whether the results were reproducible.
words.englishDefinition: able to be repeated by others under the same conditions with the same results

exampleSentences: Visiting the shrine on New Year's Day is a ritual that millions of Japanese families follow.
The graduation ceremony is a ritual marking the transition from student life to adulthood.
words.englishDefinition: a set of actions performed in a fixed order, often with cultural or religious significance

exampleSentences: Discussing salaries openly is still a taboo in many workplaces.
Mental health was once a taboo subject, but attitudes have shifted considerably.
words.englishDefinition: a social or cultural prohibition against something considered offensive or unacceptable

words.reading: マルティカルチュラリズム
words.jpTranslation: 多文化主義(たぶんかしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Canada's policy of multiculturalism encourages different ethnic groups to keep their traditions.
Critics argue that multiculturalism sometimes makes social integration more difficult.
words.englishDefinition: the principle of supporting and celebrating multiple cultural identities within a single society

words.jpTranslation: 先住民の(せんじゅうみんの)
exampleSentences: Indigenous communities in the Amazon have protected vast areas of rainforest for centuries.
The government passed laws to recognize indigenous land rights that had been ignored for decades.
words.englishDefinition: originating naturally in a particular region, especially describing peoples native to a place before colonization

exampleSentences: The skateboarding subculture has its own fashion, language, and values distinct from the mainstream.
Online gaming has grown into a global subculture with millions of dedicated followers.
words.englishDefinition: a group within a larger culture that shares distinct values, practices, or interests of its own

words.reading: カウンターカルチャー
words.jpTranslation: 対抗文化(たいこうぶんか)
exampleSentences: The 1960s counterculture rejected mainstream values and demanded civil rights and peace.
Punk music emerged as a counterculture response to the polished pop music of the era.
words.englishDefinition: a movement whose values and lifestyle directly oppose those of the dominant mainstream culture

words.jpTranslation: 大衆文化(たいしゅうぶんか)
exampleSentences: K-pop has become a major part of global pop culture, attracting fans across dozens of countries.
Teachers sometimes use references to pop culture to make lessons more engaging for students.
words.englishDefinition: the mainstream cultural trends, entertainment, and ideas widely shared by the general public

words.reading: グローバリゼーション・エフェクト
exampleSentences: One globalization effect is that local food traditions are being replaced by fast-food chains worldwide.
The globalization effect on wages has benefited some workers while leaving others behind.
words.englishDefinition: the impact or consequence that global integration of economies, cultures, and trade has on societies

words.reading: アキュルチュレーション
words.jpTranslation: 文化変容(ぶんかへんよう)
exampleSentences: Moving abroad as a teenager accelerated her acculturation into the local community.
Acculturation does not mean losing one's original identity but adapting to a new environment.
words.englishDefinition: the process of adopting elements of another culture while interacting with it over time

words.reading: カルチュラル・エクスチェンジ
words.jpTranslation: 文化交流(ぶんかこうりゅう)
exampleSentences: The student exchange program promoted cultural exchange between Japan and Australia.
Cultural exchange through food, music, and art helps break down misunderstandings between nations.
words.englishDefinition: the sharing of customs, ideas, and traditions between people from different cultural backgrounds

exampleSentences: Mass media shapes public opinion by deciding which stories receive the most coverage.
Before the internet, mass media like television and newspapers dominated information flow.
words.englishDefinition: communication channels such as television, newspapers, and online platforms that reach large audiences

words.jpTranslation: 消費主義(しょうひしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Consumerism encourages people to keep buying new products even when old ones still work.
Many young people are pushing back against consumerism by choosing secondhand clothing.
words.englishDefinition: a social and economic order that encourages buying goods and services in ever-increasing amounts

words.jpTranslation: 物質主義(ぶっしつしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: She realized that her materialism left her feeling empty despite owning many expensive things.
Materialism drives people to judge others by what they own rather than who they are.
words.englishDefinition: the tendency to consider the acquisition of material possessions as the most important goal in life

words.jpTranslation: 世俗主義(せぞくしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Secularism ensures that government policies are not based on any single religion.
France's strong tradition of secularism means religious symbols are banned in public schools.
words.englishDefinition: the principle that public life and government should be separate from religious influence

words.reading: ファンダメンタリズム
words.jpTranslation: 原理主義(げんりしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Religious fundamentalism can lead people to reject scientific findings that conflict with their beliefs.
Scholars debate whether fundamentalism is a response to modernization or a separate phenomenon.
words.englishDefinition: strict adherence to the literal interpretation of a religion's or ideology's core beliefs, often rejecting modern values

words.jpTranslation: 国際的な(こくさいてきな)
exampleSentences: London is one of the world's most cosmopolitan cities, with residents from over 100 countries.
Her cosmopolitan upbringing gave her the ability to feel at home in almost any culture.
words.englishDefinition: familiar with and at ease in many different cultures and countries; internationally minded

words.jpTranslation: 自文化中心主義(じぶんかちゅうしんしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Ethnocentrism made him dismiss foreign customs as simply wrong.
Her anthropology course challenged the ethnocentrism she had grown up with.
words.englishDefinition: the tendency to judge other cultures by the standards of one's own

words.reading: カルチュラル・リラティビズム
words.jpTranslation: 文化相対主義(ぶんかそうたいしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Cultural relativism asks us to understand practices within their own social context.
Critics argue that cultural relativism can make it hard to condemn human rights abuses.
words.englishDefinition: the view that a culture's beliefs should be understood on its own terms, not by outside standards

words.reading: アイデンティティ・ポリティクス
words.jpTranslation: アイデンティティ政治
exampleSentences: Identity politics shifted the campaign focus toward the concerns of minority voters.
Some scholars warn that identity politics can fragment a broader coalition.
words.englishDefinition: political activity that prioritizes the interests of groups defined by shared social identities such as race or gender

exampleSentences: The documentary challenged the official narrative about the city's founding.
Her novel weaves a narrative of immigration and belonging across three generations.
words.englishDefinition: a story or account that gives meaning and structure to a series of events

exampleSentences: Public discourse on climate change has grown sharper over the past decade.
The professor analyzed how media shapes the discourse around immigration.
words.englishDefinition: the way a topic is discussed, written about, and understood within a society

words.reading: ソーシャル・コウヒージョン
words.jpTranslation: 社会的結束(しゃかいてきけっそく)
exampleSentences: Shared festivals strengthen social cohesion in diverse neighborhoods.
Rising inequality has weakened social cohesion in several European cities.
words.englishDefinition: the bonds of trust, shared values, and mutual support that hold a community together

words.reading: ジェネレーショナル・ギャップ
words.jpTranslation: 世代間ギャップ(せだいかんギャップ)
exampleSentences: The generational gap around smartphone use created friction between parents and teenagers.
Policymakers struggle to bridge the generational gap in retirement savings expectations.
words.englishDefinition: the difference in attitudes, values, and experiences between people of different age groups

words.reading: ライト・オブ・パッセイジ
words.jpTranslation: 通過儀礼(つうかぎれい)
exampleSentences: In many cultures, a first solo journey serves as a rite of passage into adulthood.
Passing the university entrance exam felt like a rite of passage for him.
words.englishDefinition: a ceremony or experience that marks an important transition from one life stage to another

words.reading: ソーシャル・モビリティ
words.jpTranslation: 社会的流動性(しゃかいてきりゅうどうせい)
exampleSentences: Access to quality education is one of the strongest drivers of social mobility.
Social mobility has stalled in countries where wealth is increasingly concentrated.
words.englishDefinition: the ability of individuals or families to move up or down the social and economic ladder

exampleSentences: Each generation inherits both the achievements and the problems of the one before it.
This generation of students grew up with social media as a constant presence.
words.englishDefinition: all the people born and living around the same time, sharing similar experiences

words.reading: トラディション・バーサス・モダニティ
words.jpTranslation: 伝統と近代性(でんとうときんだいせい)
exampleSentences: The debate over tradition vs modernity shapes how many Asian societies approach family roles.
Her novel explores tradition vs modernity through a young woman who leaves her village for Tokyo.
words.englishDefinition: the tension between preserving established customs and embracing contemporary change

words.reading: ソーシャル・ストラティフィケーション
words.jpTranslation: 社会階層化(しゃかいかいそうか)
exampleSentences: Social stratification in the country is reflected in access to healthcare and schools.
Researchers measured social stratification by comparing income across five occupational categories.
words.englishDefinition: the division of society into hierarchical layers based on wealth, status, or power

words.reading: カルチュラル・インペリアリズム
words.jpTranslation: 文化帝国主義(ぶんかていこくしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Critics see the dominance of Hollywood films abroad as a form of cultural imperialism.
Cultural imperialism can quietly replace local languages with a dominant global one.
words.englishDefinition: the imposition of one culture's values, media, and practices over another, usually by a more powerful nation

words.reading: シビック・エンゲイジメント
words.jpTranslation: 市民参加(しみんさんか)
exampleSentences: Civic engagement among young voters rose sharply after the environmental protests.
Local councils rely on civic engagement to identify which road repairs are most urgent.
words.englishDefinition: active participation by citizens in the political and community life of their society

words.reading: フォーク・カルチャー
words.jpTranslation: 民俗文化(みんぞくぶんか)
exampleSentences: The region's folk culture includes handwoven textiles passed down through family workshops.
Scholars worry that folk culture disappears when rural youth move to cities for work.
words.englishDefinition: the traditional customs, crafts, and art forms practiced by ordinary people in a local community

words.reading: カルチュラル・アイデンティティ
words.jpTranslation: 文化的アイデンティティ(ぶんかてきアイデンティティ)
exampleSentences: Living abroad for years made her appreciate her cultural identity in ways she hadn't expected.
Language is often the most visible marker of cultural identity within a diaspora community.
distractors: 個人のアイデンティティ
国家のアイデンティティ
words.englishDefinition: the sense of belonging to a particular culture, shaped by shared language, history, and values

exampleSentences: Growing up in a fishing village gave him a worldview centered on patience and nature.
Reading foreign literature can gently challenge the worldview a reader takes for granted.
words.englishDefinition: a comprehensive framework of beliefs through which a person understands and interprets the world

words.reading: カルチュラル・ノーム
words.jpTranslation: 文化的規範(ぶんかてききはん)
exampleSentences: Removing shoes before entering a home is a deeply held cultural norm in Japan.
Advertising campaigns sometimes clash with local cultural norms and cause unexpected offense.
words.englishDefinition: an unwritten rule about behavior that is considered normal and expected within a particular culture

words.reading: アート・ムーブメント
words.jpTranslation: 芸術運動(げいじゅつうんどう)
exampleSentences: Impressionism began as an art movement that rejected the strict rules of academic painting.
The Harlem Renaissance was an art movement that celebrated African American culture and identity.
words.englishDefinition: a shared style or philosophy adopted by a group of artists during a particular period

exampleSentences: The Irish diaspora spread across North America, Australia, and beyond during the famine years.
Members of the Ethiopian diaspora send remittances home that support entire villages.
words.englishDefinition: a scattered population that has moved away from its original homeland and lives in multiple countries

exampleSentences: English serves as the lingua franca at international scientific conferences.
Swahili became the lingua franca of trade across much of East Africa centuries ago.
words.englishDefinition: a language used as a common means of communication between people who speak different native languages

words.jpTranslation: 二言語使用(にげんごしよう)
exampleSentences: Studies suggest that bilingualism can delay the onset of certain cognitive decline conditions.
Canada's official bilingualism means federal services must be available in both English and French.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to speak two languages fluently, or a policy of using two languages within a society

words.reading: クロス・カルチュラル
words.jpTranslation: 異文化間の(いぶんかかんの)
exampleSentences: The team's cross-cultural training helped them avoid misunderstandings during negotiations in Seoul.
Her research takes a cross-cultural approach, comparing child-rearing practices in six countries.
words.englishDefinition: involving or comparing two or more different cultures

words.reading: インターカルチュラル
words.jpTranslation: 文化間の(ぶんかかんの)
exampleSentences: The exchange program fosters intercultural understanding between Japanese and Brazilian students.
Intercultural competence is increasingly listed as a required skill in multinational job postings.
words.englishDefinition: relating to communication or interaction between people from different cultural backgrounds

words.reading: フォーク・リリジョン
words.jpTranslation: 民間信仰(みんかんしんこう)
exampleSentences: Many villagers practice a blend of Buddhism and folk religion at local shrines.
Anthropologists documented how folk religion rituals change when communities migrate to cities.
words.englishDefinition: popular religious beliefs and practices that exist alongside or outside official religious institutions

exampleSentences: The opening ceremony of the summit was watched by millions around the world.
A simple tea ceremony can take years of practice to perform with full precision.
words.englishDefinition: a formal event performed on an important occasion, following a set sequence of actions

exampleSentences: The harvest festival draws thousands of visitors who come to sample local food and music.
Edinburgh's summer festival transforms the entire city into an open-air stage for three weeks.
words.englishDefinition: a public celebration, often held annually, featuring cultural, religious, or artistic events

exampleSentences: Norse mythology describes a world-ending battle called Ragnarök involving gods and giants.
She used Greek mythology to explain how ancient societies understood natural phenomena like storms.
words.englishDefinition: a collection of traditional stories about gods, heroes, and the origins of the world in a particular culture

exampleSentences: Stoic philosophy teaches that we should focus only on what lies within our control.
Her philosophy of leadership emphasized listening before making any major decision.
words.englishDefinition: the study of fundamental questions about existence, knowledge, ethics, and how we should live

exampleSentences: Anthropology helped him understand why gift-giving rituals differ so dramatically across cultures.
She chose anthropology because she wanted to study how languages shape social relationships.
words.englishDefinition: the scientific study of human societies, cultures, and their development across time

exampleSentences: Sociology revealed that the dropout rate was strongly linked to neighborhood poverty levels.
He turned to sociology to explain why certain social movements gain momentum while others fade.
words.englishDefinition: the scientific study of human society, social institutions, and group behavior

words.reading: インフラストラクチャー
exampleSentences: Heavy rains exposed how badly the city's aging infrastructure needed investment.
Reliable internet infrastructure is now considered as essential as roads or electricity.
words.englishDefinition: the basic physical systems — such as roads, power grids, and water supply — that a society depends on

exampleSentences: Morning congestion on the expressway added forty minutes to his daily drive.
The city introduced a congestion charge to reduce the number of cars entering downtown.
words.englishDefinition: a situation where traffic or crowds are so dense that movement slows significantly

words.jpTranslation: 通勤する(つうきんする)
exampleSentences: He decided to commute by bicycle after the train fare increased for the third year in a row.
A ninety-minute commute each way left her exhausted before the workday even began.
words.englishDefinition: to travel regularly between home and a workplace or school

words.reading: アーバン・プランニング
words.jpTranslation: 都市計画(としけいかく)
exampleSentences: Good urban planning ensured that every new district had parks within walking distance.
Failures in urban planning led to neighborhoods where residents had no access to fresh food.
words.englishDefinition: the process of designing and organizing land use, transport, and public spaces in cities and towns

words.reading: パブリック・トランスポート
words.jpTranslation: 公共交通機関(こうきょうこうつうきかん)
exampleSentences: Affordable public transport allows low-income workers to reach jobs across the city.
The government invested heavily in public transport to cut car emissions by 2030.
words.englishDefinition: buses, trains, and other vehicles available for use by the general public, usually at a set fare

exampleSentences: The subway is so punctual that locals set their watches by it during rush hour.
Building a new subway line under the river required five years of tunneling work.
words.englishDefinition: an underground electric railway that runs through a city

words.jpTranslation: 高速道路(こうそくどうろ)
exampleSentences: The highway narrows to two lanes just before the mountain tunnel, causing regular backups.
Solar panels installed along the highway generate enough electricity to power nearby streetlights.
words.englishDefinition: a major road designed for high-speed travel between cities or regions

exampleSentences: The city widened sidewalks after a pedestrian was struck by a delivery van at that corner.
A pedestrian-only zone in the shopping district increased foot traffic for local businesses.
words.englishDefinition: a person who is traveling on foot, especially in an area with vehicle traffic

exampleSentences: Every cyclist on that road must wear a helmet under the new traffic regulations.
A dedicated lane separated the cyclist from fast-moving buses and taxis.
words.englishDefinition: a person who rides a bicycle, especially as a regular means of transport

words.reading: エミッション・コントロール
words.jpTranslation: 排気規制(はいききせい)
exampleSentences: Stricter emission control rules forced the auto industry to redesign its diesel engines.
Cities with robust emission control programs report measurably cleaner air than a decade ago.
words.englishDefinition: regulations and technologies that limit the amount of pollutants released by vehicles or industrial processes

exampleSentences: The smart city collects real-time data from sensors to adjust traffic signals automatically.
Residents of the smart city can report broken streetlights through an app linked to city services.
words.englishDefinition: an urban area that uses digital technology and data to improve infrastructure, services, and quality of life

exampleSentences: The new apartment complex was required to include green space on its rooftop.
Research shows that access to green space reduces stress and improves mental health in urban dwellers.
words.englishDefinition: an area of grass, trees, or other vegetation within or near a built-up area

words.reading: アーバン・スプロール
words.jpTranslation: 都市の拡大(としのかくだい)
exampleSentences: Urban sprawl pushed the city's edge forty kilometers into what had been farmland.
Planners promoted higher-density housing to slow urban sprawl and protect the surrounding forest.
words.englishDefinition: the uncontrolled expansion of urban areas into surrounding rural or natural land

exampleSentences: The bridge connecting the two islands cut the ferry journey from two hours to ten minutes.
Engineers inspected every cable on the suspension bridge after the unusually strong earthquake.
words.englishDefinition: a structure built to span a gap such as a river or road, allowing people or vehicles to cross

exampleSentences: The tunnel beneath the Alps reduced the mountain crossing from six hours to thirty minutes.
Workers discovered an old water pipe while digging the tunnel under the harbor.
words.englishDefinition: an underground or underwater passage built for road, rail, or pedestrian use

words.jpTranslation: 交通手段(こうつうしゅだん)
exampleSentences: Packages sent by air transit typically arrive within two business days.
The city's rapid transit network was expanded to connect outer suburbs to the city center.
words.englishDefinition: the act of passing through or being transported across a place, or a system for doing so

exampleSentences: Most of the country's electronics are delivered as freight by container ships from Asia.
Rail freight is cheaper per tonne than road transport for long-distance bulk goods.
words.englishDefinition: goods transported in bulk by truck, ship, train, or plane for commercial purposes

exampleSentences: The port handles over a million shipping containers every year, making it one of Asia's busiest.
After the storm, debris blocked the port entrance and delayed cargo ships for three days.
words.englishDefinition: a town or city with a harbor where ships load and unload goods or passengers

exampleSentences: The airport added a third runway to handle the surge in international flights.
She arrived at the airport two hours early but still almost missed her connecting flight.
words.englishDefinition: a place where aircraft take off and land, equipped with terminals for passengers and facilities for cargo

exampleSentences: The high-speed railway between the two capitals cut travel time from five hours to ninety minutes.
A century-old railway line was restored and now carries tourists through the mountain valley.
words.englishDefinition: a system of tracks along which trains run to transport passengers or goods

exampleSentences: An overturned lorry on the ring road caused traffic to back up for twelve kilometers.
City officials monitor traffic flow through cameras at every major junction.
words.englishDefinition: vehicles moving along roads, or the movement and volume of vehicles in a particular area

exampleSentences: Electric vehicles now account for one in five new car sales in the country.
Any vehicle over 3.5 tonnes must use the outer lane on that stretch of motorway.
words.englishDefinition: a machine used to transport people or goods from one place to another

exampleSentences: Airlines are searching for sustainable fuel alternatives to reduce their carbon footprint.
The truck ran out of fuel on a remote mountain road and had to wait hours for help.
words.englishDefinition: a substance burned or converted to produce energy for engines, heating, or power generation

words.jpTranslation: 通行料(つうこうりょう)
exampleSentences: Drivers pay a toll at the bridge entrance, and the fee doubles during peak hours.
The government raised the highway toll to fund repairs to crumbling overpasses.
words.englishDefinition: a charge paid to use a road, bridge, or tunnel

exampleSentences: The new traffic lights at the intersection reduced accidents by forty percent in one year.
He waited at the intersection while a long freight train crossed the road in front of him.
words.englishDefinition: a point where two or more roads meet and cross each other

exampleSentences: Replacing the traffic lights with a roundabout kept vehicles moving more smoothly.
She missed her exit on the roundabout and had to go all the way around a second time.
words.englishDefinition: a circular road junction where traffic moves continuously around a central island

exampleSentences: Thousands of commuters were stranded when a signal failure shut down the main line at rush hour.
The new express service cut ten minutes off a typical commuter's morning journey.
words.englishDefinition: a person who travels regularly between home and a workplace or school, often over a considerable distance

words.jpTranslation: 都市の衰退(としのすいたい)
exampleSentences: Vacant factories became symbols of urban decay after the steelworks closed in the 1980s.
Community gardens have helped reverse urban decay in several neglected downtown blocks.
words.englishDefinition: the deterioration of a city or neighborhood, marked by abandoned buildings, poverty, and declining services

words.reading: ジェントリフィケーション
words.jpTranslation: ジェントリフィケーション
exampleSentences: Gentrification pushed long-time residents out as rents tripled within five years.
Local artists who sparked the neighborhood's revival were ironically priced out by gentrification.
words.englishDefinition: the process by which wealthier residents move into a poorer urban area, raising property values and displacing existing communities

words.reading: アフォーダブル・ハウジング
words.jpTranslation: 低廉住宅(ていれんじゅうたく)
exampleSentences: The city council approved a plan to build 500 affordable housing units near the train station.
Many young workers cannot rent near their jobs because affordable housing in the area has almost disappeared.
words.englishDefinition: housing that people with low or average incomes can reasonably afford to rent or buy

words.jpTranslation: ゾーニング・用途地域
exampleSentences: Zoning rules in this neighbourhood prohibit building factories near schools.
The developer applied for a zoning change to convert the warehouse into apartments.
words.englishDefinition: a system of laws that divides land into areas designated for specific uses such as housing, industry, or commerce

exampleSentences: The historic library closed for a year during its full renovation.
After the renovation, the old factory became a bright co-working space.
words.englishDefinition: the process of repairing and improving a building so it is in better condition

exampleSentences: Residents gathered to watch the demolition of the abandoned stadium on Saturday morning.
The demolition of the old bridge will begin once the new one is open to traffic.
words.englishDefinition: the act of knocking down or destroying a building or structure completely

words.jpTranslation: 高層ビル(こうそうビル)
exampleSentences: The new skyscraper downtown will be the tallest building in the country at 450 metres.
From the top floor of the skyscraper, you can see the entire bay on a clear day.
words.englishDefinition: an extremely tall building with many floors, typically found in city centres

words.reading: レジデンシャル・エリア
exampleSentences: The council voted to keep the streets in the residential area closed to heavy trucks.
Property prices in that residential area have risen sharply since the new subway line opened.
words.englishDefinition: a part of a town or city where people live, as opposed to where businesses or factories are located

words.reading: コマーシャル・ディストリクト
words.jpTranslation: 商業地区(しょうぎょうちく)
exampleSentences: The commercial district attracts thousands of shoppers and tourists every weekend.
Rents in the central commercial district have doubled over the past five years.
words.englishDefinition: an area of a city where shops, offices, and businesses are concentrated

words.reading: ペデストリアン・ゾーン
words.jpTranslation: 歩行者天国(ほこうしゃてんごく)
exampleSentences: The mayor turned the old market street into a pedestrian zone, banning all vehicles.
Cafés and street performers have made the pedestrian zone the most vibrant part of town.
words.englishDefinition: a street or area reserved exclusively for people on foot, where vehicles are not allowed

words.jpTranslation: 自転車専用レーン(じてんしゃせんようレーン)
exampleSentences: Commuters feel much safer since the city painted a dedicated cycle lane along the main road.
Drivers who park in the cycle lane face an immediate fine under the new traffic rules.
words.englishDefinition: a marked section of a road set aside for cyclists, separate from motor vehicle traffic

words.reading: エレクトリック・スクーター
words.jpTranslation: 電動スクーター(でんどうスクーター)
exampleSentences: He rents an electric scooter from the app to cover the last kilometre to his office.
The city introduced speed limits for electric scooters after several accidents on footpaths.
words.englishDefinition: a battery-powered two-wheeled vehicle that riders stand on, used for short urban trips

words.reading: ライド・シェアリング
exampleSentences: Ride-sharing services have cut the number of cars driven solo during rush hour significantly.
She splits the cost with two colleagues through a ride-sharing app, saving money every week.
words.englishDefinition: a service that connects passengers heading in the same direction so they can share a single vehicle and split the fare

exampleSentences: The farmer switched to natural predators instead of pesticide to protect his apple orchard.
Runoff carrying pesticide from the fields polluted the nearby river and harmed the fish population.
words.englishDefinition: a chemical substance used to kill insects, weeds, or other organisms that damage crops

exampleSentences: The cultivation of rice in flooded paddies has shaped the landscape of Southeast Asia for centuries.
New greenhouse technology allows the cultivation of strawberries throughout the winter months.
words.englishDefinition: the practice of preparing land and growing crops or plants

exampleSentences: Unusually heavy rain delayed the wheat harvest by nearly three weeks this autumn.
Farmers use combine machines to harvest hundreds of acres of corn in a single day.
words.englishDefinition: the process of gathering ripe crops from the fields at the end of a growing season

exampleSentences: The prolonged drought forced ranchers to sell their livestock because there was no grass left.
Keeping livestock such as cattle and pigs accounts for a large share of global greenhouse gas emissions.
words.englishDefinition: farm animals such as cattle, sheep, and pigs that are raised for food or other agricultural purposes

exampleSentences: Without an irrigation system, growing vegetables in the desert would be virtually impossible.
Drip irrigation delivers water directly to plant roots, using far less water than traditional sprinklers.
words.englishDefinition: the artificial supply of water to land or crops through channels, pipes, or sprinklers to support plant growth

exampleSentences: Excess fertilizer washed into the lake caused an algal bloom that killed much of the fish.
Compost made from kitchen scraps works as a gentle fertilizer that slowly releases nutrients into the soil.
words.englishDefinition: a natural or chemical substance added to soil to supply nutrients that help plants grow

words.reading: クロップ・ローテーション
exampleSentences: By practising crop rotation, the farmer avoided depleting any single nutrient from his fields.
Crop rotation with legumes naturally restores nitrogen to the soil without synthetic fertilizer.
words.englishDefinition: the practice of growing different crops in the same field in successive seasons to maintain soil health and reduce pests

words.reading: サステイナブル・ファーミング
words.jpTranslation: 持続可能な農業(じぞくかのうなのうぎょう)
exampleSentences: Sustainable farming methods protect biodiversity while still producing enough food for local markets.
The government offered grants to encourage young farmers to adopt sustainable farming practices.
words.englishDefinition: agricultural practices designed to produce food without depleting natural resources or harming the environment for future generations

words.reading: ジェネティカリー・モディファイド
words.jpTranslation: 遺伝子組み換えの(いでんしくみかえの)
exampleSentences: Genetically modified maize was developed to resist a beetle that previously destroyed entire harvests.
Some consumers refuse to buy genetically modified produce, preferring traditional varieties instead.
words.englishDefinition: (of an organism) having had its DNA altered through biotechnology to introduce traits not found in the original

words.reading: フード・セキュリティ
words.jpTranslation: 食料安全保障(しょくりょうあんぜんほしょう)
exampleSentences: Climate change threatens food security in regions that already struggle with unpredictable rainfall.
The UN report warned that rising prices could push millions of people into food insecurity within a decade.
words.englishDefinition: the condition in which all people have reliable access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food

words.jpTranslation: 食品廃棄物(しょくひんはいきぶつ)
exampleSentences: Supermarkets are reducing food waste by donating unsold items to charities each evening.
About one-third of all food produced globally is lost to food waste before it reaches a dinner table.
words.englishDefinition: edible food that is discarded or left to spoil rather than being eaten

words.reading: オーガニック・ファーミング
words.jpTranslation: 有機農業(ゆうきのうぎょう)
exampleSentences: Organic farming bans synthetic pesticides, relying instead on crop diversity and natural pest control.
Consumers often pay a premium for vegetables grown through certified organic farming methods.
words.englishDefinition: a method of agriculture that avoids synthetic chemicals and uses natural processes to grow crops and raise animals

words.reading: ドラウト・レジスタント
words.jpTranslation: 干ばつ耐性の(かんばつたいせいの)
exampleSentences: Scientists developed a drought-resistant wheat variety that yields well even with very little rain.
Planting drought-resistant shrubs in the garden reduces the need for regular watering during summer.
words.englishDefinition: (of a plant or crop) able to survive and grow with very little water or during periods of prolonged dry weather

words.reading: サブシスタンス・ファーミング
words.jpTranslation: 自給農業(じきゅうのうぎょう)
exampleSentences: Many rural families in the region rely on subsistence farming, growing just enough food to feed themselves.
Subsistence farming leaves little surplus to sell, so cash income remains extremely limited.
words.englishDefinition: a form of agriculture in which farmers grow only enough food for their own consumption, with little or nothing left to sell

words.jpTranslation: 水産養殖(すいさんようしょく)
exampleSentences: Salmon raised through aquaculture now supplies more than half the salmon sold in supermarkets worldwide.
Aquaculture in coastal lagoons can damage local ecosystems if waste from fish cages is not managed carefully.
words.englishDefinition: the controlled breeding and raising of fish, shellfish, or aquatic plants in water for food

words.jpTranslation: 耕作可能な(こうさくかのうな)
exampleSentences: Only a small fraction of the country's land is arable; the rest is too rocky or dry to cultivate.
Converting forest into arable land often causes serious environmental damage to surrounding ecosystems.
words.englishDefinition: (of land) suitable for plowing and growing crops

words.jpTranslation: 収穫量(しゅうかくりょう)
exampleSentences: Better soil management raised the farm's wheat yield by thirty percent in just two seasons.
A late frost in April dramatically cut the apple yield, pushing prices up at the market.
words.englishDefinition: the total amount of a crop produced from a piece of land in a growing season

words.jpTranslation: 農業ビジネス(のうぎょうビジネス)
exampleSentences: Large agribusiness companies control most of the seed and fertilizer supply in the global market.
Critics argue that the rise of agribusiness has driven small family farms out of existence.
words.englishDefinition: agriculture conducted on an industrial, commercial scale, including farming, food processing, and distribution as an integrated business

words.jpTranslation: 食物連鎖(しょくもつれんさ)
exampleSentences: When a top predator disappears, it disrupts the entire food chain below it.
Microplastics consumed by tiny fish enter the food chain and eventually reach the fish we eat.
words.englishDefinition: a sequence of organisms in which each feeds on the one below it, transferring energy from plants through animals

words.reading: マルニュートリション
words.jpTranslation: 栄養失調(えいようしっちょう)
exampleSentences: Children suffering from malnutrition often show stunted growth and weakened immune systems.
Malnutrition can result not only from too little food but also from diets that lack essential vitamins.
words.englishDefinition: a condition caused by not getting the right amount or balance of nutrients the body needs to stay healthy

exampleSentences: Volunteers at the food bank sort and pack donated groceries for hundreds of families every week.
Demand at the local food bank tripled after the factory closed and unemployment in the town rose sharply.
words.englishDefinition: a place where donated food is collected and distributed free of charge to people who cannot afford to buy it

exampleSentences: As a vegan, she avoids all animal products, including dairy, eggs, and honey.
The restaurant added several vegan options to its menu after noticing growing demand from customers.
words.englishDefinition: a person who does not eat or use any animal products whatsoever, including meat, dairy, and eggs

exampleSentences: He became a vegetarian after learning about the environmental impact of meat production.
The school cafeteria now offers a vegetarian lunch option every day of the week.
words.englishDefinition: a person who does not eat meat or fish, though they may consume dairy products and eggs

words.jpTranslation: 食品安全(しょくひんあんぜん)
exampleSentences: Inspectors closed the restaurant after it repeatedly failed food safety checks on hygiene standards.
Proper refrigeration is one of the most basic food safety measures for preventing bacterial growth.
words.englishDefinition: the practices and regulations that ensure food is handled, prepared, and stored in ways that prevent illness

exampleSentences: Heavy processing strips many grains of the fibre and vitamins naturally present in whole form.
The food processing plant employs over three hundred workers who package and freeze vegetables daily.
words.englishDefinition: the series of mechanical or chemical steps used to transform raw ingredients into packaged food products

words.reading: ディストリビューション
exampleSentences: An efficient cold-chain distribution network ensures fresh produce reaches stores before it spoils.
Poor distribution infrastructure means that food rots in rural warehouses while cities face shortages.
words.englishDefinition: the process of delivering goods from producers to shops, markets, or consumers

words.reading: エクスポート・クロップ
words.jpTranslation: 輸出作物(ゆしゅつさくもつ)
exampleSentences: Coffee became the country's most valuable export crop, generating billions in foreign currency each year.
Dependence on a single export crop makes an economy very vulnerable to global price fluctuations.
words.englishDefinition: a crop grown primarily to sell to other countries rather than for domestic consumption

exampleSentences: The new land use policy restricts development in wetland areas to protect biodiversity.
Poor land use planning has led to urban sprawl that encroaches on valuable agricultural areas.
words.englishDefinition: the way in which land is managed and the purpose for which it is used, such as farming, housing, or industry

words.reading: ソイル・ディグレデーション
words.jpTranslation: 土壌劣化(どじょうれっか)
exampleSentences: Overgrazing by cattle accelerates soil degradation, leaving the ground bare and prone to erosion.
Soil degradation costs farmers billions annually in lost productivity and remediation expenses.
words.englishDefinition: the decline in soil quality and fertility caused by erosion, overuse, pollution, or poor farming practices

exampleSentences: Rapid urban expansion has swallowed thousands of hectares of farmland on the city's outskirts.
The government introduced subsidies to help young farmers purchase farmland at affordable prices.
words.englishDefinition: land that is used or suitable for growing crops or raising animals

exampleSentences: Skipping sleep regularly has serious consequences for both memory and physical health.
She understood that every financial decision she made would have long-term consequences for her family.
words.englishDefinition: a result or effect that follows from an action or condition, often negative

exampleSentences: Under normal circumstances, the flight would take two hours, but bad weather added another ninety minutes.
His generous circumstances allowed him to pursue art full-time without worrying about money.
words.englishDefinition: a fact or condition that affects a situation or event and often lies beyond someone's control

exampleSentences: Viral video trends are a fascinating social phenomenon that spread across borders within hours.
Scientists are still debating the exact cause of the strange atmospheric phenomenon observed last winter.
words.englishDefinition: an observable fact or event, especially one that is unusual, interesting, or not yet fully explained

exampleSentences: The main criterion for selecting finalists was the originality of their proposed solutions.
Price alone cannot be the sole criterion when choosing a university; location and course quality matter too.
words.englishDefinition: a standard or principle used to judge, evaluate, or make a decision about something

exampleSentences: The study's implication is that screen time before bed significantly disrupts teenagers' sleep cycles.
Policymakers must consider the economic implications of raising the minimum wage before acting.
words.englishDefinition: a conclusion or consequence that is suggested by something but not directly stated

exampleSentences: It is a paradox that the more connected we become digitally, the lonelier many people report feeling.
The paradox of choice suggests that having too many options can actually make decision-making harder.
words.englishDefinition: a statement or situation that seems contradictory yet turns out to express a deeper truth

exampleSentences: She faced a dilemma: accept the higher-paid job abroad or stay close to her ageing parents.
The doctor's ethical dilemma arose when the patient refused treatment that could have saved his life.
words.englishDefinition: a situation in which a difficult choice must be made between two or more equally undesirable options

words.reading: コントラディクション
exampleSentences: There is a clear contradiction between the company's environmental pledges and its record levels of emissions.
He noticed the contradiction in her argument and asked her to clarify which position she actually held.
words.englishDefinition: a combination of statements or ideas that are logically inconsistent with each other

exampleSentences: The contract's ambiguity over payment dates led to a dispute between the two companies.
Good legal writing aims to eliminate ambiguity so that no clause can be interpreted in two different ways.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being open to more than one interpretation, making the meaning unclear or uncertain

exampleSentences: The complexity of the immigration system makes it difficult for applicants to navigate without a lawyer.
She appreciated the complexity of the novel's plot, which revealed new layers of meaning on each reading.
words.englishDefinition: the state of having many interconnected parts or factors that make something difficult to understand or deal with

words.jpTranslation: 不確実性(ふかくじつせい)
exampleSentences: Economic uncertainty following the election caused many businesses to delay their investment plans.
Living with uncertainty about his health diagnosis was the hardest part of waiting for the test results.
words.englishDefinition: the state of not knowing what will happen or what the correct answer is; lack of predictability

words.jpTranslation: 両面価値(りょうめんかち)
exampleSentences: Her ambivalence about moving abroad was clear — she was excited about the opportunity but scared to leave her friends.
The public's ambivalence toward the new technology showed up in polls that were almost evenly split.
words.englishDefinition: the state of having mixed or conflicting feelings about someone or something at the same time

words.reading: パーセプション・バーサス・リアリティ
words.jpTranslation: 認識と現実(にんしきとげんじつ)
exampleSentences: The gap between perception vs reality often explains why people fear flying more than driving, despite the statistics.
Marketing campaigns frequently exploit the difference between perception vs reality to make ordinary products seem extraordinary.
words.englishDefinition: the contrast between how something is believed or imagined to be and how it actually is

exampleSentences: The irony of the situation was that the fire station itself burned down during the drought.
There is a bitter irony in the fact that the region's best farming land now lies under a shopping centre.
words.englishDefinition: a situation or expression in which the actual outcome or meaning is opposite to what was expected or intended

exampleSentences: Describing the brain as a computer is a useful metaphor, but it can mislead people about how memory actually works.
The poet used the metaphor of a crumbling wall to capture the slow collapse of the couple's relationship.
words.englishDefinition: a figure of speech that describes something by saying it is something else, highlighting a resemblance without using 'like' or 'as'

exampleSentences: The teacher used the analogy of water flowing through pipes to explain how electricity moves through a circuit.
Drawing an analogy between learning a language and learning to drive helped the students understand the role of practice.
words.englishDefinition: a comparison between two different things that share a similar structure, used to explain or clarify an idea

exampleSentences: Once carbon emissions cross a certain threshold, some climate tipping points may become irreversible.
The noise level in the office exceeded the legal threshold, so management installed sound-absorbing panels.
words.englishDefinition: the level or point at which something begins to happen or have an effect

exampleSentences: The country's transition from a military government to democracy took over a decade.
Many students struggle with the transition from high school to university life.
words.englishDefinition: a change or shift from one state, condition, or period to another

words.reading: トランスフォーメーション
exampleSentences: The neighborhood underwent a dramatic transformation after the new transit line opened.
His transformation from a shy teenager into a confident speaker surprised everyone.
words.englishDefinition: a thorough or radical change in form, character, or condition

exampleSentences: The industrial revolution moved millions of workers from farms into factories.
Social media sparked a revolution in how ordinary people share news and opinions.
words.englishDefinition: a fundamental and often rapid overthrow of a system, government, or way of doing things

exampleSentences: The architect's legacy lives on in the dozen public buildings she designed across the city.
Colonialism left a complex legacy that still shapes politics in many countries today.
words.englishDefinition: something handed down from the past, such as an achievement, impact, or consequence

exampleSentences: The court ruling set a precedent that judges in lower courts must now follow.
There is no precedent for a company of this size filing for bankruptcy so quickly.
words.englishDefinition: an earlier event or decision that serves as an example or rule for future cases

exampleSentences: There is growing tension between economic growth goals and environmental protection.
Political tension along the border escalated after the disputed election results.
words.englishDefinition: a state of conflict, strain, or unresolved opposition between two forces or groups

exampleSentences: She struggled to find a balance between caring for her children and advancing her career.
A healthy diet requires a balance of proteins, carbohydrates, and fats.
words.englishDefinition: a stable distribution of weight, importance, or attention between two or more things

exampleSentences: After months of price swings, the housing market finally reached a new equilibrium.
The ecosystem lost its equilibrium when an invasive species was introduced.
words.englishDefinition: a state of balance in which opposing forces are equal and the system is stable

exampleSentences: The campaign gained momentum after a popular video went viral on social media.
Once the project lost momentum, it was hard to get the team motivated again.
words.englishDefinition: the strength or force that keeps something moving forward or developing

exampleSentences: The gap in test scores between rich and poor schools has widened over the past decade.
There is a significant gap between what politicians promise and what they deliver.
