words.englishDefinition: a difference or disparity between two things, groups, or levels

exampleSentences: After the popular café closed, nothing filled the social void it left in the community.
The sudden resignation of the CEO created a leadership void no one was ready to fill.
words.englishDefinition: an empty space or a complete absence of something previously present

exampleSentences: For centuries, the dominant paradigm placed Earth at the center of the universe.
Remote work has challenged the traditional paradigm that productivity requires an office.
words.englishDefinition: a widely accepted model or way of thinking that shapes how people understand a subject

exampleSentences: The two candidates hold very different ideologies on the role of government in the economy.
Extremist ideology spreads more easily when people feel economically or socially excluded.
words.englishDefinition: a system of ideas and beliefs that forms the basis of political or social thinking

exampleSentences: The novel imagines a utopia where energy is unlimited and no one goes hungry.
Critics argue that any attempt to build a utopia risks becoming authoritarian in practice.
words.englishDefinition: an imagined perfect society where everything functions ideally and everyone is happy

exampleSentences: The film depicts a dystopia where corporations control every aspect of daily life.
Many readers see warnings of real-world dystopia in novels like '1984' and 'Brave New World'.
words.englishDefinition: an imagined society where conditions are extremely bad, oppressive, or dehumanizing

words.jpTranslation: 抽象化(ちゅうしょうか)
exampleSentences: The philosophy lecture was full of abstraction, making it hard to connect ideas to real life.
Programming relies on abstraction to hide complex details behind simple interfaces.
words.englishDefinition: the process of representing something in a general or simplified way, removed from specific detail

words.jpTranslation: 象徴主義(しょうちょうしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: The white dove is used as symbolism for peace in many cultures around the world.
The author's use of darkness and light as symbolism reinforces the story's moral themes.
words.englishDefinition: the use of objects, colors, or images to represent deeper meanings or ideas

words.jpTranslation: 物語の弧(ものがたりのこ)
exampleSentences: The film's narrative arc follows the hero from failure through growth to eventual redemption.
A strong narrative arc keeps readers engaged because they can sense the story is moving somewhere.
words.englishDefinition: the overarching shape of a story, tracing how characters and events develop from beginning to end

exampleSentences: The wise mentor is a common archetype found in myths and stories across many cultures.
Marketing teams sometimes build customer personas around recognizable archetypes.
words.englishDefinition: a very typical example of a person or thing that serves as a universal model or pattern

exampleSentences: Employees who are given greater autonomy over their schedules tend to report higher job satisfaction.
The region demanded autonomy from the central government to manage its own education system.
words.englishDefinition: the right or ability to make one's own decisions and act independently

exampleSentences: Protesters questioned the legitimacy of an election marred by widespread irregularities.
A new government must earn legitimacy by delivering on its promises to citizens.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being accepted as lawful, justified, or recognized as valid by others

words.jpTranslation: 説明責任(せつめいせきにん)
exampleSentences: Citizens demanded accountability from officials who misused public funds.
Clear reporting structures improve accountability within large organizations.
words.englishDefinition: the obligation to explain one's actions and accept responsibility for their outcomes

exampleSentences: The judge was known for her integrity, refusing gifts and treating all parties equally.
Admitting the mistake publicly took courage and showed real integrity.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being honest and having strong moral principles that one consistently follows

exampleSentences: The scientist's credibility was damaged when the data in her paper was found to be inaccurate.
A news organization builds credibility over years but can lose it with a single false report.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being trusted and believed by others based on past reliability or expertise

exampleSentences: Researchers questioned the validity of the survey because the sample was too small.
The court examined the validity of the contract before ruling on the dispute.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being logically sound, well-grounded, or legally acceptable

exampleSentences: Engineers tested the reliability of the bridge design under extreme weather conditions.
Passengers lost confidence in the airline's reliability after repeated delays.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of performing consistently and dependably under expected conditions

exampleSentences: The chef's consistency in flavor is what keeps customers returning every week.
Lack of consistency in grading made students unsure what standards they were being judged by.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of behaving or performing in the same way over time, without unexpected variation

exampleSentences: The essay lacked coherence because each paragraph introduced a new idea without connecting to the last.
A policy plan needs internal coherence so that its goals do not contradict each other.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of forming a unified, logical, and clearly connected whole

exampleSentences: Teachers must demonstrate the relevance of history lessons to students' everyday lives.
The document was excluded from court because its relevance to the case could not be established.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being closely connected to or appropriate for the matter being considered

exampleSentences: Investors asked about the app's scalability before committing to fund the next growth phase.
Cloud infrastructure improves scalability because computing power can be added on demand.
words.englishDefinition: the capacity of a system or plan to grow and handle increased demand without losing performance

words.jpTranslation: 実現可能性(じつげんかのうせい)
exampleSentences: The engineers conducted a feasibility study before committing to the underwater tunnel project.
Doubts about financial feasibility forced the city to abandon the stadium plan.
words.englishDefinition: the likelihood that something can be successfully done given available resources and conditions

words.jpTranslation: 持続可能性(じぞくかのうせい)
exampleSentences: Consumers are increasingly choosing brands that demonstrate environmental sustainability.
The charity questioned the long-term sustainability of funding programs through one-time donations.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to continue over the long term without depleting resources or causing lasting harm

exampleSentences: Adaptability is one of the most valued traits in workers whose industries change rapidly.
The species survived because of its adaptability to a wide range of climates and food sources.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to adjust successfully to new conditions or changing circumstances

words.jpTranslation: 柔軟性(じゅうなんせい)
exampleSentences: The contract includes flexibility to renegotiate terms if market conditions change significantly.
Scheduling flexibility is one reason many workers prefer freelancing over fixed office jobs.
words.englishDefinition: the willingness or ability to change or be changed easily to suit different situations

exampleSentences: The advertising campaign stood out because of its creativity in turning an ordinary product into a story.
Schools that allow unstructured play time often see greater creativity in students' problem-solving.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to produce original ideas or works that are imaginative and inventive

words.jpTranslation: 臨界量(りんかいりょう)
exampleSentences: The app needed a critical mass of users before advertisers were willing to invest.
Once the protest movement reached critical mass, politicians could no longer ignore its demands.
words.englishDefinition: the minimum size or number needed for something to become self-sustaining or produce a significant effect

words.reading: ティッピング・ポイント
exampleSentences: Climate scientists warn that rising temperatures could push ecosystems past a tipping point.
The factory closure was the tipping point that turned local frustration into organized protest.
words.englishDefinition: the moment when a situation reaches a threshold and begins to change rapidly and irreversibly

words.reading: リップル・エフェクト
words.jpTranslation: 波及効果(はきゅうこうか)
exampleSentences: The factory's closure had a ripple effect on restaurants, shops, and other local businesses.
A single act of kindness can create a ripple effect, inspiring others to help their neighbors.
words.englishDefinition: a spreading chain of consequences triggered by a single event, reaching further than expected

words.reading: チェイン・リアクション
words.jpTranslation: 連鎖反応(れんさはんのう)
exampleSentences: The bank's collapse triggered a chain reaction that froze credit markets across the region.
One missed deadline set off a chain reaction of delays that pushed the launch back by months.
words.englishDefinition: a sequence of events where each one causes the next, creating a self-continuing series of effects

exampleSentences: Without better education, children born into poverty often repeat the same cycle as their parents.
The business cycle moves through expansion, peak, recession, and recovery in recurring patterns.
words.englishDefinition: a series of events that repeat in the same order, returning to the starting point

words.reading: フィードバック・ループ
words.jpTranslation: フィードバックループ
exampleSentences: Customer reviews create a feedback loop that pushes companies to improve their products.
Arctic ice loss creates a feedback loop: less ice means more heat absorbed, which melts more ice.
words.englishDefinition: a process where the output of a system feeds back in as input, amplifying or regulating further change

words.jpTranslation: 悪循環(あくじゅんかん)
exampleSentences: Skipping sleep leads to poor focus, which causes more stress, trapping students in a vicious cycle.
Many developing countries are trapped in a vicious cycle where debt limits spending on growth.
words.englishDefinition: a self-reinforcing sequence where each problem makes the next one worse, with no easy exit

words.reading: バーチャス・サイクル
words.jpTranslation: 好循環(こうじゅんかん)
exampleSentences: Good reviews attract new customers, boosting revenue for better products — a classic virtuous cycle.
Education creates a virtuous cycle: more skilled workers raise productivity and fund better schools.
words.englishDefinition: a self-reinforcing sequence where each positive outcome leads to another, compounding improvement

exampleSentences: The discovery that the Earth orbited the Sun triggered a paradigm shift in astronomy.
Remote work represented a paradigm shift in how companies think about productivity and office space.
words.englishDefinition: a fundamental change in the dominant assumptions or framework that defines a field or society

exampleSentences: Giving students agency over their learning topics increases both motivation and retention.
Victims of the policy felt they had no agency in decisions that directly affected their lives.
words.englishDefinition: the capacity to act independently and make one's own choices in a given situation

words.reading: インテンショナリティ
exampleSentences: Effective leaders approach meetings with intentionality, setting clear goals before each session.
The design team praised the film for the intentionality behind every camera angle and color choice.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of doing something with clear purpose and deliberate awareness of one's goals

exampleSentences: Language is an example of emergence — complex communication arising from simple social interactions.
The emergence of a new political movement surprised analysts who had dismissed early protests.
words.englishDefinition: the process by which complex patterns or properties arise from simpler interactions within a system

words.reading: インターコネクテッドネス
words.jpTranslation: 相互連結性(そうごれんけつせい)
exampleSentences: The pandemic revealed the interconnectedness of global supply chains when one factory shut down.
Environmental education emphasizes the interconnectedness of ecosystems and human activity.
words.englishDefinition: the state of being linked together so that each part affects and depends on the others

words.jpTranslation: 全体的な(ぜんたいてきな)
exampleSentences: The clinic takes a holistic approach to health, considering diet, stress, and sleep alongside medication.
A holistic reading of the novel reveals themes that are invisible when studying individual chapters.
words.englishDefinition: considering a whole system or person rather than focusing on separate parts in isolation

exampleSentences: Her nuanced reading of the poem caught meanings most students had missed.
The documentary gave a nuanced portrait of immigrants that avoided easy stereotypes.
words.englishDefinition: showing subtle distinctions and fine shades of meaning or feeling

exampleSentences: There is a subtle irony in writing a book about the dangers of social media.
The chef made a subtle adjustment to the seasoning that transformed the dish.
words.englishDefinition: so slight or delicate as to be difficult to notice or define

words.jpTranslation: 本質的な(ほんしつてきな)
exampleSentences: Students who read for pleasure have intrinsic motivation that no reward can replace.
The intrinsic value of honesty goes beyond any practical benefit it might bring.
words.englishDefinition: belonging naturally to something as a core or essential part of it

words.jpTranslation: 外発的な(がいはつてきな)
exampleSentences: Bonuses are extrinsic motivators, but they rarely sustain long-term effort.
Researchers found that extrinsic pressure actually reduced creative performance.
words.englishDefinition: coming from outside rather than from within; not part of something's essential nature

words.jpTranslation: 具体的な・有形の(ゆうけいの)
exampleSentences: After six months of negotiations, the two countries finally made tangible progress toward peace.
Volunteers wanted tangible proof that their donations were reaching flood victims.
words.englishDefinition: real and concrete; able to be clearly seen, touched, or measured

exampleSentences: Trust is an intangible asset that companies can lose in a single scandal.
She gained something intangible from traveling alone — a quiet confidence in herself.
words.englishDefinition: unable to be physically touched or precisely measured, yet genuinely real

words.jpTranslation: 測定可能な(そくていかのうな)
exampleSentences: The new tutoring program produced measurable gains in reading scores within one term.
Set measurable goals so you can track your own progress week by week.
words.englishDefinition: able to be assessed or quantified using a clear standard or scale

words.reading: クォンティファイアブル
words.jpTranslation: 定量化できる(ていりょうかできる)
exampleSentences: Customer satisfaction is harder to assess than quantifiable data like sales figures.
Carbon emissions are quantifiable, which makes climate targets easier to enforce legally.
words.englishDefinition: expressible as a specific number or amount that can be counted or calculated

words.reading: インヒアレント・テンション
words.jpTranslation: 内在する緊張(ないざいするきんちょう)
exampleSentences: There is an inherent tension between protecting free speech and preventing online harassment.
The policy debate revealed an inherent tension between economic growth and environmental protection.
words.englishDefinition: a conflict or contradiction that exists naturally within a system or between two principles

exampleSentences: Working remotely offers flexibility, but the trade-off is fewer opportunities to collaborate.
Cheaper ingredients lower costs, yet the trade-off is often a drop in product quality.
words.englishDefinition: a balance between two desirable but incompatible things, where gaining one means losing some of the other

exampleSentences: The dichotomy between science and religion has been debated for centuries.
Critics argued that the dichotomy between rich and poor had grown sharper over the decade.
words.englishDefinition: a division into two sharply contrasting or mutually exclusive categories

exampleSentences: Her essay offered a bold synthesis of evolutionary biology and cultural theory.
The album is a synthesis of jazz, classical music, and electronic sound design.
words.englishDefinition: the combining of different ideas, elements, or disciplines into a coherent whole

exampleSentences: His critique of the government's housing policy appeared in three national newspapers.
The professor offered a thorough critique of each student's argument, praising clarity but questioning the evidence.
words.englishDefinition: a detailed and reasoned analysis that identifies both strengths and weaknesses

exampleSentences: Epistemology asks not just what we know, but how we can claim to know anything at all.
The seminar examined how cultural background shapes epistemology and what counts as evidence.
words.englishDefinition: the branch of philosophy concerned with the nature, sources, and limits of knowledge

exampleSentences: Ontology grapples with the most basic question: what kinds of things actually exist?
In computer science, an ontology maps the relationships between concepts in a specific domain.
words.englishDefinition: the branch of philosophy that studies the fundamental nature of existence and being

exampleSentences: Medical ethics requires doctors to respect a patient's right to refuse treatment.
The scandal raised serious questions about ethics in the financial industry.
words.englishDefinition: the study or set of moral principles that guide right and wrong conduct

exampleSentences: The architect's aesthetics favored clean lines and natural light over ornate decoration.
Philosophers of aesthetics debate whether beauty is in the object or the observer's eye.
words.englishDefinition: the study of beauty, taste, and the principles underlying artistic judgment

words.reading: サブジェクティビティ
exampleSentences: Film critics openly admit that subjectivity shapes every review they write.
The judge acknowledged the inherent subjectivity in scoring a figure-skating performance.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being based on personal feelings, opinions, or interpretation rather than facts

words.reading: オブジェクティビティ
words.jpTranslation: 客観性(きゃっかんせい)
exampleSentences: Journalists strive for objectivity, but their word choices inevitably reflect some bias.
Good science depends on objectivity — data must not be filtered to support a preferred conclusion.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being uninfluenced by personal feelings and based on observable facts

exampleSentences: Cultural relativity suggests that practices should be understood within their own social context.
Einstein's theory of relativity redefined how physicists think about time, space, and gravity.
words.englishDefinition: the principle that judgments or measurements are not absolute but depend on context or a reference point

words.jpTranslation: 絶対主義(ぜったいしゅぎ)
exampleSentences: Moral absolutism holds that certain actions — such as torture — are always wrong, regardless of outcome.
Political absolutism concentrates all authority in a single ruler who faces no legal limits.
words.englishDefinition: the belief that certain principles or rules are unconditionally true or that power should be unlimited

exampleSentences: The speech's allusion to Martin Luther King was unmistakable to anyone familiar with the civil rights movement.
Without catching the allusion to Greek mythology, many readers missed the whole irony of the novel's ending.
words.englishDefinition: an indirect reference to a person, event, or work that the audience is expected to recognise

exampleSentences: From the wet umbrella by the door, she drew the inference that someone had just come in from the rain.
Reading comprehension tests often check whether students can make inferences beyond what is stated directly.
words.englishDefinition: a conclusion reached by reasoning from evidence rather than from direct observation

exampleSentences: Sherlock Holmes is famous for his rapid deduction — starting from general principles to identify the only possible suspect.
By deduction, scientists concluded the planet must exist even before they could observe it directly.
words.englishDefinition: the process of reaching a specific conclusion by applying a general rule or principle

exampleSentences: After observing hundreds of swans, the scientist used induction to conclude that all swans in the region were white.
Induction builds theories from repeated observations, but a single counterexample can overturn the whole pattern.
words.englishDefinition: the process of drawing a general conclusion from a series of specific observations or cases

exampleSentences: The entire argument collapses if you challenge its central premise that people always act rationally.
She questioned the premise of the study, pointing out that the sample was far too small.
words.englishDefinition: a statement or assumption treated as a starting point from which a conclusion is drawn

words.jpTranslation: 先入観を持って判断する
exampleSentences: It is unfair to prejudge a candidate before reviewing their full portfolio of work.
Jurors are instructed not to prejudge the defendant's guilt before hearing all the testimony.
words.englishDefinition: to form an opinion about someone or something before knowing or examining the full facts

words.reading: コグニティブ・ロード
exampleSentences: Cluttered slide designs increase cognitive load and make it harder to absorb the speaker's message.
Experienced nurses handle high cognitive load in emergencies because routine tasks have become automatic.
words.englishDefinition: the total amount of mental effort required to process information at a given moment

exampleSentences: "Buy local" is a useful heuristic when you lack time to research every food product's full supply chain.
Doctors use fever as a heuristic for infection, though it can also signal other conditions.
words.englishDefinition: a practical mental shortcut or rule of thumb that helps reach a good-enough decision quickly

exampleSentences: Her intuition told her something was wrong long before the financial report confirmed the losses.
Years of clinical experience give doctors an intuition that pure textbook knowledge cannot provide.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to understand or know something immediately, without conscious reasoning

words.jpTranslation: 理論的根拠(りろんてきこんきょ)
exampleSentences: The committee asked the designers to present the rationale behind their choice of materials.
Without a clear rationale, the policy change confused staff and led to inconsistent decisions.
words.englishDefinition: the set of reasons or logical basis underlying a decision, belief, or course of action

words.reading: ジャスティフィケーション
exampleSentences: The manager offered no justification for cutting the team's budget by forty percent.
Philosophers debate whether self-defense can ever serve as a justification for using lethal force.
words.englishDefinition: a reason or explanation that shows an action or belief to be correct or acceptable

words.reading: アカウンタビリティ・ギャップ
words.jpTranslation: 説明責任の欠如(せつめいせきにんのけつじょ)
exampleSentences: When police misconduct goes unpunished, the accountability gap erodes public trust in law enforcement.
The audit exposed an accountability gap — no one knew who had approved the controversial spending.
words.englishDefinition: a situation in which people or institutions that hold power cannot be held responsible for their actions

words.reading: パワー・ダイナミクス
words.jpTranslation: 権力関係(けんりょくかんけい)
exampleSentences: The power dynamics between a patient and a doctor make it difficult for patients to question medical advice.
Understanding power dynamics in a workplace helps employees navigate conflicts more effectively.
words.englishDefinition: the ways in which power is distributed and exercised among people or groups in a relationship or situation

words.reading: ソーシャル・コントラクト
words.jpTranslation: 社会契約(しゃかいけいやく)
exampleSentences: Paying taxes is part of the social contract — citizens fund services that benefit everyone.
Some argue that rising inequality has broken the social contract that once held communities together.
words.englishDefinition: the unwritten agreement in which individuals give up some freedoms in exchange for order and protection from society

words.reading: コレクティブ・アクション
words.jpTranslation: 集団行動(しゅうだんこうどう)
exampleSentences: Reducing carbon emissions requires collective action — no single country can solve the problem alone.
Workers organised collective action, refusing overtime until management agreed to better safety conditions.
words.englishDefinition: coordinated effort by a group working together to achieve a shared goal that individuals cannot reach alone

exampleSentences: A free rider benefits from public transport improvements without contributing any taxes toward them.
The free rider problem makes it hard to fund environmental protection through voluntary donations.
words.englishDefinition: someone who enjoys the benefits of a collective effort or public resource without contributing their fair share

words.jpTranslation: 公共財(こうきょうざい)
exampleSentences: National defence is a public good — protecting one citizen automatically protects all others.
Governments fund public goods like street lighting because private companies have little incentive to do so.
words.englishDefinition: a resource or service that is available to everyone and whose use by one person does not reduce availability for others

exampleSentences: Air pollution from a factory is a negative externality borne by nearby residents who had no say in the decision.
A well-educated workforce is a positive externality — businesses benefit from schools they did not fund.
words.englishDefinition: a cost or benefit that affects a third party who was not involved in the decision that caused it

words.reading: オポチュニティ・コスト
words.jpTranslation: 機会費用(きかいひよう)
exampleSentences: Spending an evening watching TV has an opportunity cost — you could have been studying or exercising.
The opportunity cost of attending university full-time is the salary you forgo by not working.
words.englishDefinition: the value of the best alternative you give up when making a choice

words.reading: ディミニッシング・リターンズ
words.jpTranslation: 収穫逓減(しゅうかくていげん)
exampleSentences: After the fourth coffee of the day, she noticed diminishing returns — it no longer helped her concentrate.
Adding more workers to an already crowded production line quickly runs into diminishing returns.
words.englishDefinition: the point at which adding more of something produces smaller and smaller additional benefits

words.reading: エコノミーズ・オブ・スケール
words.jpTranslation: 規模の経済(きぼのけいざい)
exampleSentences: Large supermarket chains achieve economies of scale by buying goods in vast quantities at lower unit costs.
Start-ups struggle to compete with established rivals that already enjoy economies of scale.
words.englishDefinition: cost advantages that a company gains by increasing its level of production, making each unit cheaper to produce

words.reading: マーケット・フェイラー
words.jpTranslation: 市場の失敗(しじょうのしっぱい)
exampleSentences: The financial crisis of 2008 was widely cited as evidence of a catastrophic market failure.
Environmental regulation is justified when pollution represents a market failure that prices do not correct.
words.englishDefinition: a situation in which the free market allocates resources inefficiently, leading to poor social outcomes

words.reading: コンパラティブ・アドバンテージ
words.jpTranslation: 比較優位(ひかくゆうい)
exampleSentences: Vietnam's comparative advantage in textile manufacturing stems from lower labour costs and skilled workers.
Countries benefit from trade by specialising in goods where they have a comparative advantage.
words.englishDefinition: the ability of a person or country to produce something at a lower relative cost than others, making trade mutually beneficial

words.jpTranslation: 弾力性(だんりょくせい)
exampleSentences: Demand for petrol has low elasticity — drivers still buy it even when prices rise sharply.
The elasticity of demand for luxury handbags drops during a recession as consumers cut spending.
words.englishDefinition: the degree to which supply or demand changes in response to a change in price or other economic factor

words.reading: イクイリブリウム・プライス
words.jpTranslation: 均衡価格(きんこうかかく)
exampleSentences: When housing supply finally meets demand, the market settles on an equilibrium price that clears the backlog.
A sudden drought disrupted the equilibrium price of wheat, sending grocery costs sharply upward.
words.englishDefinition: the price at which the quantity of a good that sellers want to supply equals the quantity buyers want to purchase

exampleSentences: The restaurant's profit margin shrank when ingredient costs doubled over the summer.
The company operates on a razor-thin margin, so even small price changes can push it into loss.
words.englishDefinition: the difference between the selling price of a product and the cost of producing or acquiring it

exampleSentences: The school used national test scores as a benchmark to evaluate whether its students were keeping pace.
This new processor sets a benchmark that competitors will struggle to match for at least two years.
words.englishDefinition: a standard or reference point used to measure or compare performance and quality

words.jpTranslation: マイルストーン・節目
exampleSentences: Signing the trade agreement was a milestone in the two countries' thirty-year diplomatic relationship.
The team celebrated reaching one million users, a milestone they had once thought impossible.
words.englishDefinition: a significant event or achievement that marks an important stage in a process or journey

exampleSentences: The project plan listed each deliverable along with a deadline and the name of the responsible team member.
The client was unhappy because two key deliverables had been submitted weeks behind schedule.
words.englishDefinition: a specific output or result that must be completed and handed over as part of a project or contract

words.reading: ステークホルダー・アナリシス
words.jpTranslation: 利害関係者分析(りがいかんけいしゃぶんせき)
exampleSentences: Before launching the policy, officials conducted a stakeholder analysis to identify who would be affected and how.
The stakeholder analysis revealed that local farmers opposed the dam project far more strongly than planners had assumed.
words.englishDefinition: the process of identifying people or groups with an interest in a project and understanding their concerns and influence

words.reading: デュー・ディリジェンス
exampleSentences: The investors spent three months conducting due diligence before agreeing to acquire the startup.
Skipping due diligence to close the deal quickly proved to be a costly mistake.
words.englishDefinition: the careful research and verification of facts carried out before making a significant decision or investment

words.reading: プルーフ・オブ・コンセプト
words.jpTranslation: 概念実証(がいねんじっしょう)
exampleSentences: The engineers built a small proof of concept to show investors the technology actually worked.
Before committing full funding, the board asked for a proof of concept that could be tested in the field.
words.englishDefinition: a small-scale demonstration that shows a proposed idea or method is feasible and worth developing further

words.reading: パイロット・スタディ
words.jpTranslation: パイロット研究(パイロットけんきゅう)
exampleSentences: Researchers ran a pilot study with thirty participants to check whether the questionnaire was clearly worded.
The pilot study identified several logistical problems that would have derailed the main experiment.
words.englishDefinition: a small-scale preliminary study carried out to test methods and identify issues before a full-scale investigation

words.reading: コスト・ベネフィット・アナリシス
words.jpTranslation: 費用便益分析(ひようべんえきぶんせき)
exampleSentences: The city council commissioned a cost-benefit analysis before deciding whether to build the new bridge.
A careful cost-benefit analysis showed that investing in early childhood education saves money on social services later.
words.englishDefinition: a systematic calculation comparing the total costs of an action against its expected benefits to judge whether it is worthwhile

words.reading: リスク・アセスメント
words.jpTranslation: リスク評価(リスクひょうか)
exampleSentences: The construction team completed a risk assessment before breaking ground near the historic riverbank.
A thorough risk assessment flagged the supplier's financial instability before any contracts were signed.
words.englishDefinition: a formal process of identifying potential hazards and evaluating how likely and how serious their consequences might be

words.reading: コンティンジェンシー・プラン
words.jpTranslation: 緊急時対応計画(きんきゅうじたいおうけいかく)
exampleSentences: The event organisers prepared a contingency plan in case heavy rain forced them to move indoors.
Having a contingency plan meant the team kept operating smoothly when the lead developer fell ill.
words.englishDefinition: a backup strategy prepared in advance to handle a situation if the original plan fails or an unexpected event occurs

words.reading: クォリティ・アシュアランス
words.jpTranslation: 品質保証(ひんしつほしょう)
exampleSentences: The factory introduced quality assurance checks to catch defects before shipping.
Our software team runs quality assurance tests after every code update.
words.englishDefinition: the process of checking that a product or service meets required standards before it reaches the customer

words.reading: フィードバック・メカニズム
words.jpTranslation: フィードバックメカニズム
exampleSentences: The app's star-rating system acts as a feedback mechanism that guides future improvements.
Without a reliable feedback mechanism, managers cannot know whether changes are working.
words.englishDefinition: a system that collects responses to a process and uses them to adjust or improve it

exampleSentences: Each iteration of the design brought the prototype closer to what users actually wanted.
After five iterations, the team finally produced a stable version of the software.
words.englishDefinition: one complete cycle of a repeated process, especially in design or software development

words.reading: プロトタイプ・テスティング
exampleSentences: Prototype testing revealed that the button was too small for younger users to tap.
The engineers spent two weeks on prototype testing before approving the final design.
words.englishDefinition: the process of evaluating an early model of a product to find problems before full production

words.reading: ユーザー・エクスペリエンス
words.jpTranslation: ユーザーエクスペリエンス
exampleSentences: Slow loading times ruin the user experience, causing visitors to leave the site immediately.
The redesigned checkout page dramatically improved the user experience for mobile shoppers.
words.englishDefinition: the overall feeling a person has while interacting with a product, website, or service

words.reading: デザイン・シンキング
words.jpTranslation: デザイン思考(デザインしこう)
exampleSentences: Using design thinking, the nurses reimagined the hospital waiting area from a patient's perspective.
Design thinking encourages teams to prototype quickly and learn from real user reactions.
words.englishDefinition: a creative problem-solving approach that centres on deeply understanding users' needs

words.reading: アジャイル・メソドロジー
words.jpTranslation: アジャイル手法(アジャイルしゅほう)
exampleSentences: Agile methodology lets developers release small updates every two weeks rather than waiting months.
The startup adopted agile methodology so it could respond quickly to customer complaints.
words.englishDefinition: an iterative approach to project management that delivers work in short cycles and welcomes change

exampleSentences: During the two-week sprint, the team focused solely on rebuilding the login screen.
At the end of each sprint, developers demo what they built to the product owner.
words.englishDefinition: a fixed, short period of work in agile development during which a specific set of tasks must be completed

exampleSentences: The product owner sorted the backlog so the most requested features were tackled first.
After the holiday break, the support team faced a backlog of over 400 unread tickets.
words.englishDefinition: a prioritised list of tasks or features that a team has yet to complete

exampleSentences: In the sprint retrospective, the team agreed that daily check-ins had taken too long.
A honest retrospective helps colleagues identify what slowed them down and fix it next time.
words.englishDefinition: a structured meeting held after a project or sprint to reflect on what went well and what should change

exampleSentences: Adding a second server increased the website's throughput from 500 to 1,200 requests per second.
The factory measured throughput daily to find out how many units were being finished per hour.
words.englishDefinition: the amount of work or data processed by a system within a given period of time

exampleSentences: The slow approval process was the main bottleneck preventing the team from meeting its deadline.
Engineers discovered the bottleneck in the pipeline was a single underpowered database server.
words.englishDefinition: a point in a process where work slows down because demand exceeds capacity

exampleSentences: Automating invoice approvals simplified the finance department's workflow considerably.
A well-documented workflow means new employees can start contributing without constant guidance.
words.englishDefinition: the sequence of steps through which a task or project moves from start to completion

words.reading: オートメーション・バイアス
words.jpTranslation: 自動化バイアス(じどうかバイアス)
exampleSentences: Automation bias caused the pilot to trust the faulty sensor reading instead of looking out the window.
Researchers warn that automation bias makes people ignore their own judgment when a machine gives an answer.
words.englishDefinition: the tendency to over-rely on automated systems and accept their output without critical evaluation

exampleSentences: Scope creep turned a two-month website project into a six-month ordeal when clients kept adding features.
Project managers use detailed contracts to prevent scope creep from derailing timelines and budgets.
words.englishDefinition: the gradual expansion of a project's goals beyond what was originally agreed, without corresponding adjustments to time or budget

exampleSentences: The journalist worked through the night to submit her article before the morning deadline.
Missing the grant deadline meant the research team had to wait another year to reapply.
distractors: 余裕あるスケジュール
words.englishDefinition: the latest time or date by which something must be finished or submitted

words.reading: タイム・マネジメント
words.jpTranslation: 時間管理(じかんかんり)
exampleSentences: Good time management allowed her to finish her assignments early and still have evenings free.
The seminar on time management taught students to break large tasks into smaller daily goals.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to plan and control how one spends time in order to work efficiently

words.reading: プライオリタイゼーション
words.jpTranslation: 優先順位付け(ゆうせんじゅんいづけ)
exampleSentences: Effective prioritization meant the team fixed the security bug before adding any new features.
Without careful prioritization, you risk spending hours on minor tasks while urgent ones wait.
words.englishDefinition: the act of deciding which tasks are most important and should be done first

exampleSentences: The director's skill at delegation meant her staff grew more confident and capable each month.
Delegation freed the manager to focus on strategy while team members handled the daily operations.
words.englishDefinition: the practice of assigning responsibility for a task to another person while retaining overall accountability

exampleSentences: Financial empowerment programs taught low-income women how to open bank accounts and apply for loans.
The manager's decision to let staff choose their own hours was seen as genuine empowerment.
words.englishDefinition: the process of giving people greater authority, confidence, or resources to control their own lives or work

words.reading: リーダーシップ・スタイル
words.jpTranslation: リーダーシップスタイル
exampleSentences: Her democratic leadership style meant every team member had a voice before decisions were made.
Research shows that a coach-like leadership style tends to raise employee satisfaction in creative industries.
words.englishDefinition: the characteristic way in which a person guides and motivates others toward shared goals

words.reading: エモーショナル・インテリジェンス
words.jpTranslation: 感情知性(かんじょうちせい)
exampleSentences: High emotional intelligence helped the counsellor sense when a student was struggling even before speaking.
Leaders with strong emotional intelligence tend to handle conflict calmly rather than escalating tension.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to recognise, understand, and manage one's own emotions and respond appropriately to others'

words.reading: カルチュラル・コンピテンス
words.jpTranslation: 文化的能力(ぶんかてきのうりょく)
exampleSentences: Doctors working in diverse communities need cultural competence to give advice patients will actually follow.
The training programme built cultural competence by teaching staff about different communication norms.
words.englishDefinition: the ability to understand and interact effectively with people from different cultural backgrounds

words.reading: クロス・ファンクショナル
words.jpTranslation: 部門横断的な(ぶもんおうだんてきな)
exampleSentences: A cross-functional team of engineers, designers, and marketers launched the product in record time.
Cross-functional meetings help each department understand how its decisions affect the rest of the company.
words.englishDefinition: involving people from different departments or areas of expertise working together on a shared goal

words.reading: インターディシプリナリー
words.jpTranslation: 学際的な(がくさいてきな)
exampleSentences: The interdisciplinary research project combined psychology, economics, and public health to study poverty.
Her interdisciplinary degree, blending history and data science, made her stand out to employers.
words.englishDefinition: drawing on knowledge and methods from multiple academic fields to address a question

words.reading: トランスディシプリナリー
words.jpTranslation: 超学際的な(ちょうがくさいてきな)
exampleSentences: A transdisciplinary approach brought scientists, artists, and community leaders together to tackle urban flooding.
Transdisciplinary research goes beyond mixing fields; it incorporates non-academic voices from the start.
words.englishDefinition: going beyond traditional disciplinary boundaries by integrating academic and non-academic knowledge to solve complex real-world problems

exampleSentences: The album was created through collaboration between a Japanese composer and a Kenyan vocalist.
Close collaboration between the hospital and the university accelerated the development of the new treatment.
words.englishDefinition: the act of working jointly with others toward a shared outcome

words.jpTranslation: 相乗効果(そうじょうこうか)
exampleSentences: The merger created genuine synergy, with each company's strengths covering the other's weaknesses.
When the two teams pooled their data, the synergy produced insights neither could have found alone.
words.englishDefinition: the combined effect of two or more elements working together that is greater than the sum of their individual effects

words.jpTranslation: 結束力(けっそくりょく)
exampleSentences: Years of playing together gave the squad a cohesiveness that new rival teams found hard to break.
The editor rewrote the opening chapter to improve the cohesiveness of the novel's argument.
words.englishDefinition: the quality of being closely united and forming a strong, well-integrated whole

words.reading: ブレインストーミング
words.jpTranslation: ブレインストーミング
exampleSentences: Twenty minutes of brainstorming produced more campaign ideas than a week of solo thinking had.
The teacher structured the brainstorming session so that no idea was criticised until the list was complete.
words.englishDefinition: a group technique for generating many ideas freely and without immediate judgment

exampleSentences: After two hours of debate, the committee finally reached a consensus on the new zoning rules.
There is a growing scientific consensus that ultra-processed foods raise the risk of chronic disease.
words.englishDefinition: a general agreement reached by all or most members of a group

exampleSentences: The landlord and tenant reached a compromise: rent would rise slightly, but the lease would be extended.
Effective diplomacy often requires each side to compromise on less critical demands.
words.englishDefinition: an agreement in which each side gives up something it wants in order to settle a dispute

exampleSentences: After weeks of negotiation, the union and management agreed on a five percent pay rise.
Strong negotiation skills helped her secure a starting salary well above the advertised range.
words.englishDefinition: a formal discussion between parties who aim to reach a mutually acceptable agreement

exampleSentences: The neighbours avoided a costly lawsuit by settling their boundary dispute through mediation.
A trained mediator guided the mediation so both business partners could express their concerns fairly.
words.englishDefinition: a process in which a neutral third party helps two sides resolve a dispute without going to court

exampleSentences: The contract required any salary disputes to be settled by binding arbitration rather than litigation.
An independent panel was convened for arbitration after the two countries failed to agree on fishing rights.
words.englishDefinition: a process where an impartial third party hears both sides of a dispute and makes a binding decision

words.jpTranslation: 内部告発者(ないぶこくはつしゃ)
exampleSentences: The whistleblower handed thousands of internal emails to journalists, exposing the bank's fraud.
Many countries have passed laws to protect a whistleblower from being fired for reporting wrongdoing.
words.englishDefinition: a person who exposes illegal or unethical activity within an organisation to authorities or the public

words.reading: トランスパレンシー・リポート
words.jpTranslation: 透明性レポート(とうめいせいレポート)
exampleSentences: The social media company's transparency report showed it had removed over two million fake accounts last quarter.
Critics argued the transparency report omitted key details about how user data was shared with advertisers.
words.englishDefinition: a public document in which an organisation discloses data about its practices, such as government data requests or content removals

exampleSentences: Weak governance at the charity allowed executives to misuse donated funds for years undetected.
The city improved its governance by publishing all council meeting minutes online within 48 hours.
words.englishDefinition: the system of rules, practices, and processes by which an organisation or country is directed and controlled

exampleSentences: Independent oversight of police conduct helps rebuild public trust after misconduct scandals.
The committee called for stronger parliamentary oversight of the intelligence agencies' surveillance programmes.
words.englishDefinition: the supervision and monitoring of a process or organisation to ensure it operates correctly and fairly

words.reading: アカウンタビリティ・フレームワーク
exampleSentences: The UN introduced an accountability framework so member states could be measured against their climate pledges.
Without a clear accountability framework, it is impossible to know who is responsible when a project fails.
words.englishDefinition: a structured set of rules and metrics that defines who is responsible for what and how performance will be measured

words.reading: インスティテューショナル
words.jpTranslation: 制度的な(せいどてきな)
exampleSentences: Institutional racism operates through policies rather than just individual prejudice.
The university sought institutional accreditation to prove its programmes met national academic standards.
words.englishDefinition: relating to a large, established organisation or the rules and systems that govern it

words.jpTranslation: 手続き的な(てつづきてきな)
exampleSentences: The conviction was overturned on procedural grounds because the defendant had not been informed of his rights.
Procedural fairness requires that every applicant follows the same steps and is judged by the same criteria.
words.englishDefinition: relating to established rules and official steps that must be followed in a process

words.jpTranslation: 実質的な(じっしつてきな)
exampleSentences: The two leaders finally held substantive talks on nuclear disarmament rather than exchanging pleasantries.
Critics said the report lacked substantive evidence and relied too heavily on anecdotes.
words.englishDefinition: dealing with the real content or core issues of something, rather than technicalities or procedures

words.jpTranslation: 規範的な(きはんてきな)
exampleSentences: The philosopher argued that normative ethics should guide how AI systems are designed, not just describe how they behave.
Normative expectations about gender roles differ significantly across cultures and generations.
words.englishDefinition: relating to standards or rules about how things ought to be, rather than how they actually are

words.jpTranslation: 記述的な(きじゅつてきな)
exampleSentences: The geologist's report was purely descriptive, cataloguing rock types without recommending any action.
Descriptive statistics like the mean and median summarise data without making predictions.
words.englishDefinition: aimed at accurately recording or explaining something as it is, without making judgments or prescriptions

words.jpTranslation: 処方的な(しょほうてきな)
exampleSentences: The style guide is prescriptive, telling writers exactly which words and formats to use.
A prescriptive approach to grammar insists on rules even when native speakers routinely ignore them.
words.englishDefinition: giving explicit instructions or rules that tell people what they should do

words.jpTranslation: 実証的な(じっしょうてきな)
exampleSentences: The claim that the drug reduces anxiety rests on empirical evidence from three controlled trials.
Ancient Greek philosophers relied on logic, but modern scientists demand empirical proof.
words.englishDefinition: based on observation, experiment, or real-world data rather than theory alone

words.jpTranslation: 理論的な(りろんてきな)
exampleSentences: The physicist's theoretical predictions about black holes were confirmed decades later by telescope observations.
His theoretical knowledge of sailing was solid, but he had never actually handled a boat in strong wind.
words.englishDefinition: relating to ideas, models, or principles that explain something, as opposed to practical application

words.jpTranslation: 概念的な(がいねんてきな)
exampleSentences: The architect presented a conceptual sketch to convey the building's mood before drawing technical plans.
Students often grasp the conceptual framework of evolution without fully understanding its genetic mechanisms.
words.englishDefinition: relating to broad ideas or mental models rather than specific details or concrete examples

words.jpTranslation: 業務上の(ぎょうむじょうの)
exampleSentences: The new shift schedule reduced operational costs by cutting unnecessary overtime hours.
Operational challenges like supply-chain delays prevented the factory from meeting its monthly targets.
words.englishDefinition: relating to the day-to-day activities and practical running of an organisation or system

words.jpTranslation: 戦術的な(せんじゅつてきな)
exampleSentences: Switching to a three-five-two formation was a tactical choice designed to exploit the opponent's weak midfield.
The campaign made a tactical decision to focus advertising on swing states rather than safe districts.
words.englishDefinition: relating to carefully planned actions designed to achieve a specific short-term objective

words.jpTranslation: 縦断的な(じゅうだんてきな)
exampleSentences: A longitudinal study followed the same 500 children from birth to age eighteen to track their development.
Longitudinal data revealed that early reading habits strongly predicted academic success decades later.
distractors: スナップショット的な
words.englishDefinition: tracking the same subjects or data points repeatedly over a long period of time

words.reading: クロス・セクショナル
words.jpTranslation: 横断的な(おうだんてきな)
exampleSentences: The cross-sectional survey compared the fitness levels of 2,000 adults of different ages on the same day.
Cross-sectional data can reveal patterns across a population but cannot prove what caused those patterns.
words.englishDefinition: examining many different individuals or groups at a single point in time to capture a snapshot of a population

words.jpTranslation: メタ分析(メタぶんせき)
exampleSentences: The meta-analysis combined results from 40 trials and found a consistent link between sleep deprivation and obesity.
Because no single study was conclusive, researchers performed a meta-analysis to detect the true overall effect.
words.englishDefinition: a statistical technique that combines and re-analyses the results of multiple studies on the same question

words.reading: システマティック・レビュー
words.jpTranslation: システマティックレビュー
exampleSentences: The systematic review examined every published trial on the vaccine and rated the quality of each one.
Health guidelines are updated when a systematic review finds strong new evidence that contradicts older advice.
words.englishDefinition: a rigorous literature review that identifies, evaluates, and synthesises all relevant studies on a specific topic

exampleSentences: The business school used the collapse of Enron as a case study in the dangers of poor corporate governance.
A detailed case study of the town showed how relocating the highway transformed its local economy.
words.englishDefinition: an in-depth investigation of a single person, event, or organisation used to illustrate broader principles

words.reading: グラウンデッド・セオリー
words.jpTranslation: グラウンデッドセオリー
exampleSentences: Using grounded theory, the sociologist interviewed dozens of refugees before forming any hypothesis about their needs.
Grounded theory is suited to exploring new topics because the researcher lets patterns emerge from the data itself.
words.englishDefinition: a qualitative research method in which theories are built up inductively from patterns found in collected data
